revision.comment: [[WP:AES|←]]新しいページ: 「'''車持氏'''（くるまもちうじ）は「車持」を[[氏]]の名とする[[氏族]]。職業部（[[品部]]）である[[車持部]]を管掌する氏族。 == 概要 == 『[[新撰姓氏録]]』の左京皇別によると、車持公は、[[上毛野氏|上毛野朝臣]]と同じ祖先であり、[[豊城入彦命]]の8世の子孫、射狭君の末裔で、雄略天皇の時に乗與を供進したことから、「車持公」の氏姓…」
revision.text:
'''車持氏'''（くるまもちうじ）は「車持」を[[氏]]の名とする[[氏族]]。職業部（[[品部]]）である[[車持部]]を管掌する氏族。
== 概要 == 『[[新撰姓氏録]]』の左京皇別によると、車持公は、[[上毛野氏|上毛野朝臣]]と同じ祖先であり、[[豊城入彦命]]の8世の子孫、射狭君の末裔で、雄略天皇の時に乗與を供進したことから、「車持公」の氏姓を賜ったとある。
『[[日本書紀]]』には、[[履中天皇]]5年（推定[[404年]]）10月、天皇が急死した皇妃[[黒媛|葛城黒媛]]を埋葬した際に、神の祟りで妃を失ったことを悔やみ、その原因を探ったところ、車持君が[[筑紫国]]で調査した[[車持部]]をほしいままに検校し、さらに[[宗像神社]]に割き充てられていた車持部も奪い取ったことが発覚した。天皇は、この2つの罪状で車持君を糾弾し、[[摂津国]]長渚崎（ながすのざき）（現在の[[兵庫県]][[尼崎市]]長洲]あたり）で供え物と禊ぎをさせ、「今後、筑紫の車持部を管掌してはならない」とされたという<ref>『日本書紀』履中天皇5年10月11日条</ref>。この時に車持氏は内定氏族として、朝廷内に力を持っていたことが窺われる<ref>『日本古代氏族事典』</ref>。
『[[日本三代実録]]』[[元慶]]6年（[[882年]]）12月条によると、車持氏は[[律令]]体制下において[[宮内省]][[主殿寮]]の[[殿部]]に属する負名人であると見え、供御の輿輦などを職務としていたとあり、『[[延喜式]]』[[践祚]][[大嘗祭]]条には、車持朝臣の1名が菅蓋を取る役目をしたことが見えている<ref>『延喜式』巻7神祇「践祚大嘗祭」40条</ref>。
同じ『三代実録』によると、元慶元年（[[877年]]）12月25日条の[[都御酉]]の賜姓記事に、崇神天皇の子孫で、上毛野氏・大野氏・池田氏・佐味氏・車持氏と同祖であるとあり、『[[続日本紀]]』[[延暦]]10年（[[791年]]）4月の[[住吉綱主|池原綱主]]の奏上によると、「池原・上毛野の2氏は[[豊城入彦命]]より出ており、彼の子孫である東国の6氏（上述5氏のほか、[[下毛野氏]]も含まれる）の朝臣は居住地によって姓を賜り、氏を称している」とある<ref>『続日本紀』延喜10年4月5日条</ref>。このことから、車持氏を含む6氏は、東国に居住し、地名を氏族名としたことが窺われ、藤原宮跡出土木簡によると、[[上野国]][[群馬郡]]は7世紀末まで「車評」であったことが判明している。『[[国内神名帳]]』の1つである『上野国神名帳』によると、「群馬郡西郡之分」の中に、「車持明神」・「車持若御子明神」の名が見え、この地が車持氏の関与する地の一つであったことが提示されている。
[[群馬県]][[高崎市]]保渡田町の[[保渡田古墳群]]には、墳丘長100メートル前後・[[舟形石棺]]の3基の[[前方後円墳]]が存在し、ともに5世紀後半から6世紀前半の築造と推定され、上毛野国形成のはじまりとなった豪族の墓と考えられている。同時期の豪族の居館跡とされる[[三ツ寺遺跡|三ツ寺Ⅰ遺跡]]も、この地域と大和政権との繋がりを示唆している。
車持氏の[[カバネ|姓]]は[[キミ (カバネ)|君]]であり、[[天武天皇]]の[[八色の姓]]で[[朝臣]]姓になっている<ref>『日本書紀』天武天皇13年11月1日条</ref>。ほかにも[[連]]・[[首 (姓)|首]]が見られる。[[藤原不比等]]の外祖父に、車持国子君がいた、という記録も見られ<ref>『尊卑分脈』</ref>、『[[正倉院文書|東南院文書]]』の「天平宝字五年（[[761年]]）十一月廿七日大和国[[十市郡]]司売買地券文解」よると、天平宝字また車持朝臣の中には、[[平城京|平城]]右京の5条2坊を本貫とするものもいた<ref>『東南院文書』三 - 五五頁</ref>。[[長岡京]]から出土された[[墨書土器]]「車宅」を、車持氏の宅であるとする説も見られる<ref>清水みき「墨書土器『車宅』をめぐって」『長岡京古文化論叢』</ref>。
== 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}}
== 参考文献 == * 『日本書紀』（三）、[[岩波文庫]]、1994年 * 『日本書紀』（五）、[[岩波文庫]]、1995年 * [[宇治谷孟]]訳、『日本書紀』全現代語訳（上）、[[講談社]]〈[[講談社学術文庫]]〉、1988年 * 宇治谷孟訳、『日本書紀』全現代語訳（下）、講談社〈講談社学術文庫〉、1988年 *『続日本紀1 （新日本古典文学大系16）』 [[岩波書店]]、1989年 *『続日本紀5 （新日本古典文学大系16）』 岩波書店、1998年 * 宇治谷孟訳『続日本紀 （上）』講談社〈講談社学術文庫〉、1989年 * 宇治谷孟訳『続日本紀 （下）』講談社〈講談社学術文庫〉、1995年 * 『日本古代氏族事典』【新装版】[[佐伯有清]]：編212 - 213頁、[[雄山閣]]、2015年 * 『日本古代氏族人名辞典』275頁[[坂本太郎 (歴史学者)|坂本太郎]]・[[平野邦雄]]監修、[[吉川弘文館]]、1990年 *[[竹内理三]]・[[山田英雄 (日本史学者)|山田英雄]]・[[平野邦雄]]編『日本古代人名辞典』3 - 729頁、[[吉川弘文館]]、1972年
{{Japanese-history-stub}} {{people-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:くるまもちうし}} [[Category:車持氏|*]] [[Category:日本の氏族]] [[Category:近畿地方の氏族]] [[Category:氏]]

contributor.username: ja>壁掛成蛸は三考する葦である

revision.text:
'''車持氏'''（くるまもちうじ）は「車持」を[[氏]]の名とする[[氏族]]。職業部（[[品部]]）である[[車持部]]を管掌する氏族。
== 概要 == 『[[新撰姓氏録]]』の左京皇別によると、車持公は、[[上毛野氏|上毛野朝臣]]と同じ祖先であり、[[豊城入彦命]]の8世の子孫、射狭君の末裔で、雄略天皇の時に乗與を供進したことから、「車持公」の氏姓を賜ったとある。
『[[日本書紀]]』には、[[履中天皇]]5年（推定[[404年]]）10月、天皇が急死した皇妃[[黒媛|葛城黒媛]]を埋葬した際に、神の祟りで妃を失ったことを悔やみ、その原因を探ったところ、車持君が[[筑紫国]]で調査した[[車持部]]をほしいままに検校し、さらに[[宗像神社]]に割き充てられていた車持部も奪い取ったことが発覚した。天皇は、この2つの罪状で車持君を糾弾し、[[摂津国]]長渚崎（ながすのざき）（現在の[[兵庫県]][[尼崎市]]長洲]あたり）で供え物と禊ぎをさせ、「今後、筑紫の車持部を管掌してはならない」とされたという<ref>『日本書紀』履中天皇5年10月11日条</ref>。この時に車持氏は内定氏族として、朝廷内に力を持っていたことが窺われる<ref>『日本古代氏族事典』</ref>。
『日本書紀』引用の「百済本記」によれば、[[継体天皇]]3年（[[509年]]）に「[[久羅麻致支弥]]（くらまちきみ、車持君）」が[[百済]]に遣わされ、[[任那]]の日本県（やまとのあがた）の村に逃亡してきて戸籍のなくなった百済人を、本国に送還して戸籍につけたとされる<ref>コトバンク「車持君[https://kotobank.jp/word/久羅麻致支弥-1072490]」</ref>。
[[飛鳥時代]]には、[[車持国子]]の娘の[[車持与志古娘]]が[[中臣鎌足]]に嫁ぎ[[藤原不比等]]を産んだとされる。
[[奈良時代]]には、[[車持千年]]が[[宮廷歌人]]として[[元正天皇]]と[[聖武天皇]]に仕えた。
『[[日本三代実録]]』[[元慶]]6年（[[882年]]）12月条によると、車持氏は[[律令]]体制下において[[宮内省]][[主殿寮]]の[[殿部]]に属する負名人であると見え、供御の輿輦などを職務としていたとあり、『[[延喜式]]』[[践祚]][[大嘗祭]]条には、車持朝臣の1名が菅蓋を取る役目をしたことが見えている<ref>『延喜式』巻7神祇「践祚大嘗祭」40条</ref>。
同じ『三代実録』によると、元慶元年（[[877年]]）12月25日条の[[都御酉]]の賜姓記事に、崇神天皇の子孫で、上毛野氏・大野氏・池田氏・佐味氏・車持氏と同祖であるとあり、『[[続日本紀]]』[[延暦]]10年（[[791年]]）4月の[[住吉綱主|池原綱主]]の奏上によると、「池原・上毛野の2氏は[[豊城入彦命]]より出ており、彼の子孫である東国の6氏（上述5氏のほか、[[下毛野氏]]も含まれる）の朝臣は居住地によって姓を賜り、氏を称している」とある<ref>『続日本紀』延喜10年4月5日条</ref>。このことから、車持氏を含む6氏は、東国に居住し、地名を氏族名としたことが窺われ、藤原宮跡出土木簡によると、[[上野国]][[群馬郡]]は7世紀末まで「車評」であったことが判明している。『[[国内神名帳]]』の1つである『上野国神名帳』によると、「群馬郡西郡之分」の中に、「車持明神」・「車持若御子明神」の名が見え、この地が車持氏の関与する地の一つであったことが提示されている。
[[群馬県]][[高崎市]]保渡田町の[[保渡田古墳群]]には、墳丘長100メートル前後・[[舟形石棺]]の3基の[[前方後円墳]]が存在し、ともに5世紀後半から6世紀前半の築造と推定され、上毛野国形成のはじまりとなった豪族の墓と考えられている。同時期の豪族の居館跡とされる[[三ツ寺遺跡|三ツ寺Ⅰ遺跡]]も、この地域と大和政権との繋がりを示唆している。
車持氏の[[カバネ|姓]]は[[キミ (カバネ)|君]]であり、[[天武天皇]]の[[八色の姓]]で[[朝臣]]姓になっている<ref>『日本書紀』天武天皇13年11月1日条</ref>。ほかにも[[連]]・[[首 (姓)|首]]が見られる。[[藤原不比等]]の外祖父に、車持国子君がいた、という記録も見られ<ref>『尊卑分脈』</ref>、『[[正倉院文書|東南院文書]]』の「天平宝字五年（[[761年]]）十一月廿七日大和国[[十市郡]]司売買地券文解」よると、天平宝字また車持朝臣の中には、[[平城京|平城]]右京の5条2坊を本貫とするものもいた<ref>『東南院文書』三 - 五五頁</ref>。[[長岡京]]から出土された[[墨書土器]]「車宅」を、車持氏の宅であるとする説も見られる<ref>清水みき「墨書土器『車宅』をめぐって」『長岡京古文化論叢』</ref>。
== 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}}
== 参考文献 == * 『日本書紀』（三）、[[岩波文庫]]、1994年 * 『日本書紀』（五）、岩波文庫、1995年 * [[宇治谷孟]]訳『日本書紀』全現代語訳（上）、[[講談社]]〈[[講談社学術文庫]]〉、1988年 * 宇治谷孟訳『日本書紀』全現代語訳（下）、講談社〈講談社学術文庫〉、1988年 *『続日本紀1 （新日本古典文学大系16）』 [[岩波書店]]、1989年 *『続日本紀5 （新日本古典文学大系16）』 岩波書店、1998年 * 宇治谷孟訳『続日本紀 （上）』講談社〈講談社学術文庫〉、1989年 * 宇治谷孟訳『続日本紀 （下）』講談社〈講談社学術文庫〉、1995年 * 『日本古代氏族事典』【新装版】[[佐伯有清]]：編212 - 213頁、[[雄山閣]]、2015年 * 『日本古代氏族人名辞典』275頁[[坂本太郎 (歴史学者)|坂本太郎]]・[[平野邦雄]]監修、[[吉川弘文館]]、1990年 *[[竹内理三]]・[[山田英雄 (日本史学者)|山田英雄]]・[[平野邦雄]]編『日本古代人名辞典』3 - 729頁、[[吉川弘文館]]、1972年

revision.text:
'''車持氏'''（くるまもちうじ）は「車持」を[[氏]]の名とする[[氏族]]。職業部（[[品部]]）である[[車持部]]を管掌する氏族。
== 概要 == 『[[新撰姓氏録]]』の左京皇別によると、車持公は、[[上毛野氏|上毛野朝臣]]と同じ祖先であり、[[豊城入彦命]]の8世の子孫、射狭君の末裔で、雄略天皇の時に乗與を供進したことから、「車持公」の氏姓を賜ったとある。
『[[日本書紀]]』には、[[履中天皇]]5年（推定[[404年]]）10月、天皇が急死した皇妃[[黒媛|葛城黒媛]]を埋葬した際に、神の祟りで妃を失ったことを悔やみ、その原因を探ったところ、車持君が[[筑紫国]]で調査した[[車持部]]をほしいままに検校し、さらに[[宗像神社]]に割き充てられていた車持部も奪い取ったことが発覚した。天皇は、この2つの罪状で車持君を糾弾し、[[摂津国]]長渚崎（ながすのざき）（現在の[[兵庫県]][[尼崎市]]長洲]あたり）で供え物と禊ぎをさせ、「今後、筑紫の車持部を管掌してはならない」とされたという<ref>『日本書紀』履中天皇5年10月11日条</ref>。この時に車持氏は内定氏族として、朝廷内に力を持っていたことが窺われる<ref>『日本古代氏族事典』</ref>。
『日本書紀』引用の「百済本記」によれば、[[継体天皇]]3年（[[509年]]）に「[[久羅麻致支弥]]（くらまちきみ、車持君）」が[[百済]]に遣わされ、[[任那]]の日本県（やまとのあがた）の村に逃亡してきて戸籍のなくなった百済人を、本国に送還して戸籍につけたとされる<ref>コトバンク「車持君[https://kotobank.jp/word/久羅麻致支弥-1072490]」</ref>。
[[飛鳥時代]]には、[[車持国子]]の娘の[[車持与志古娘]]が[[中臣鎌足]]に嫁ぎ[[藤原不比等]]を産んだとされる。
[[奈良時代]]には、[[車持千年]]が[[宮廷歌人]]として[[元正天皇]]と[[聖武天皇]]に仕えた。
『[[日本三代実録]]』[[元慶]]6年（[[882年]]）12月条によると、車持氏は[[律令]]体制下において[[宮内省]][[主殿寮]]の[[殿部]]に属する負名人であると見え、供御の輿輦などを職務としていたとあり、『[[延喜式]]』[[践祚]][[大嘗祭]]条には、車持朝臣の1名が菅蓋を取る役目をしたことが見えている<ref>『延喜式』巻7神祇「践祚大嘗祭」40条</ref>。
同じ『三代実録』によると、元慶元年（[[877年]]）12月25日条の[[都御酉]]の賜姓記事に、崇神天皇の子孫で、上毛野氏・大野氏・池田氏・佐味氏・車持氏と同祖であるとあり、『[[続日本紀]]』[[延暦]]10年（[[791年]]）4月の[[住吉綱主|池原綱主]]の奏上によると、「池原・上毛野の2氏は[[豊城入彦命]]より出ており、彼の子孫である東国の6氏（上述5氏のほか、[[下毛野氏]]も含まれる）の朝臣は居住地によって姓を賜り、氏を称している」とある<ref>『続日本紀』延喜10年4月5日条</ref>。このことから、車持氏を含む6氏は、東国に居住し、地名を氏族名としたことが窺われ、藤原宮跡出土木簡によると、[[上野国]][[群馬郡]]は7世紀末まで「車評」であったことが判明している。『[[国内神名帳]]』の1つである『上野国神名帳』によると、「群馬郡西郡之分」の中に、「車持明神」・「車持若御子明神」の名が見え、この地が車持氏の関与する地の一つであったことが提示されている。
[[群馬県]][[高崎市]]保渡田町の[[保渡田古墳群]]には、墳丘長100メートル前後・[[舟形石棺]]の3基の[[前方後円墳]]が存在し、ともに5世紀後半から6世紀前半の築造と推定され、上毛野国形成のはじまりとなった豪族の墓と考えられている。同時期の豪族の居館跡とされる[[三ツ寺遺跡|三ツ寺Ⅰ遺跡]]も、この地域と大和政権との繋がりを示唆している。
車持氏の[[カバネ|姓]]は[[キミ (カバネ)|君]]であり、[[天武天皇]]の[[八色の姓]]で[[朝臣]]姓になっている<ref>『日本書紀』天武天皇13年11月1日条</ref>。ほかにも[[連]]・[[首 (姓)|首]]が見られる。[[藤原不比等]]の外祖父に、車持国子君がいた、という記録も見られ<ref>『尊卑分脈』</ref>、『[[正倉院文書|東南院文書]]』の「天平宝字五年（[[761年]]）十一月廿七日大和国[[十市郡]]司売買地券文解」よると、天平宝字また車持朝臣の中には、[[平城京|平城]]右京の5条2坊を本貫とするものもいた<ref>『東南院文書』三 - 五五頁</ref>。[[長岡京]]から出土された[[墨書土器]]「車宅」を、車持氏の宅であるとする説も見られる<ref>清水みき「墨書土器『車宅』をめぐって」『長岡京古文化論叢』</ref>。
== 木簡や戸籍などに見える車持氏 == *車持朝臣（名欠）、車持朝臣氏道 *:太宰府政庁遺跡から出土した木簡に名前が見える。 *車持君泥麻呂、車持君伊久沙、車持君羊 *:豊前国仲津丁里の戸主狭度勝某の戸口。伊久沙は泥麻呂の嫡子、羊は伊久沙の弟。 *車持祖麻呂、車持鷹甘 *:平城宮跡出土木簡に名前が見える。 *車持若麻呂 *:平城宮跡から出土した題箋に名前が見える。
== 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}}
== 参考文献 == * 『日本書紀』（三）、[[岩波文庫]]、1994年 * 『日本書紀』（五）、岩波文庫、1995年 * [[宇治谷孟]]訳『日本書紀』全現代語訳（上）、[[講談社]]〈[[講談社学術文庫]]〉、1988年 * 宇治谷孟訳『日本書紀』全現代語訳（下）、講談社〈講談社学術文庫〉、1988年 *『続日本紀1 （新日本古典文学大系16）』 [[岩波書店]]、1989年 *『続日本紀5 （新日本古典文学大系16）』 岩波書店、1998年 * 宇治谷孟訳『続日本紀 （上）』講談社〈講談社学術文庫〉、1989年 * 宇治谷孟訳『続日本紀 （下）』講談社〈講談社学術文庫〉、1995年 * 『日本古代氏族事典』【新装版】[[佐伯有清]]：編212 - 213頁、[[雄山閣]]、2015年 * 『日本古代氏族人名辞典』275頁[[坂本太郎 (歴史学者)|坂本太郎]]・[[平野邦雄]]監修、[[吉川弘文館]]、1990年 *[[竹内理三]]・[[山田英雄 (日本史学者)|山田英雄]]・[[平野邦雄]]編『日本古代人名辞典』3 - 729頁、[[吉川弘文館]]、1972年
{{Japanese-history-stub}} {{people-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:くるまもちうし}} [[Category:車持氏|!]] [[Category:日本の氏族]] [[Category:近畿地方の氏族]] [[Category:氏]]

『日本書紀』引用の「百済本記」によれば、[[継体天皇]]3年（[[509年]]）に「[[久羅麻致支弥]]（くらまちきみ、車持君）」が[[百済]]に遣わされ、[[任那]]の日本県（やまとのあがた）の村に逃亡してきて戸籍のなくなった百済人を、本国に送還して戸籍につけたとされる<ref>コトバンク「車持君[https://kotobank.jp/word/久羅麻致支弥-1072490]」</ref>。
[[飛鳥時代]]には、[[車持国子]]の娘の[[車持与志古娘]]が[[中臣鎌足]]に嫁ぎ[[藤原不比等]]を産んだとされる。
[[奈良時代]]には、[[車持千年]]が[[宮廷歌人]]として[[元正天皇]]と[[聖武天皇]]に仕えた。
『[[日本三代実録]]』[[元慶]]6年（[[882年]]）12月条によると、車持氏は[[律令]]体制下において[[宮内省]][[主殿寮]]の[[殿部]]に属する負名人であると見え、供御の輿輦などを職務としていたとあり、『[[延喜式]]』[[践祚]][[大嘗祭]]条には、車持朝臣の1名が菅蓋を取る役目をしたことが見えている<ref>『延喜式』巻7神祇「践祚大嘗祭」40条</ref>。
同じ『三代実録』によると、元慶元年（[[877年]]）12月25日条の[[都御酉]]の賜姓記事に、崇神天皇の子孫で、上毛野氏・大野氏・池田氏・佐味氏・車持氏と同祖であるとあり、『[[続日本紀]]』[[延暦]]10年（[[791年]]）4月の[[住吉綱主|池原綱主]]の奏上によると、「池原・上毛野の2氏は[[豊城入彦命]]より出ており、彼の子孫である東国の6氏（上述5氏のほか、[[下毛野氏]]も含まれる）の朝臣は居住地によって姓を賜り、氏を称している」とある<ref>『続日本紀』延喜10年4月5日条</ref>。このことから、車持氏を含む6氏は、東国に居住し、地名を氏族名としたことが窺われ、藤原宮跡出土木簡によると、[[上野国]][[群馬郡]]は7世紀末まで「車評」であったことが判明している。『[[国内神名帳]]』の1つである『上野国神名帳』によると、「群馬郡西郡之分」の中に、「車持明神」・「車持若御子明神」の名が見え、この地が車持氏の関与する地の一つであったことが提示されている。
[[群馬県]][[高崎市]]保渡田町の[[保渡田古墳群]]には、墳丘長100メートル前後・[[舟形石棺]]の3基の[[前方後円墳]]が存在し、ともに5世紀後半から6世紀前半の築造と推定され、上毛野国形成のはじまりとなった豪族の墓と考えられている。同時期の豪族の居館跡とされる[[三ツ寺遺跡|三ツ寺Ⅰ遺跡]]も、この地域と大和政権との繋がりを示唆している。
車持氏の[[カバネ|姓]]は[[キミ (カバネ)|君]]であり、[[天武天皇]]の[[八色の姓]]で[[朝臣]]姓になっている<ref>『日本書紀』天武天皇13年11月1日条</ref>。ほかにも[[連]]・[[首 (姓)|首]]が見られる。[[藤原不比等]]の外祖父に、車持国子君がいた、という記録も見られ<ref>『尊卑分脈』</ref>、『[[正倉院文書|東南院文書]]』の「天平宝字五年（[[761年]]）十一月廿七日大和国[[十市郡]]司売買地券文解」よると、天平宝字また車持朝臣の中には、[[平城京|平城]]右京の5条2坊を本貫とするものもいた<ref>『東南院文書』三 - 五五頁</ref>。[[長岡京]]から出土された[[墨書土器]]「車宅」を、車持氏の宅であるとする説も見られる<ref>清水みき「墨書土器『車宅』をめぐって」『長岡京古文化論叢』</ref>。
== 木簡や戸籍などに見える車持氏 == *車持朝臣（名欠）、車持朝臣氏道 *:太宰府政庁遺跡から出土した木簡に名前が見える。 *車持君泥麻呂、車持君伊久沙、車持君羊 *:「豊前国仲津郡丁里大宝二年戸籍断簡」に見える、豊前国仲津郡丁里の戸主・狭度勝某の戸口。伊久沙は泥麻呂の嫡子、羊は伊久沙の弟。 *車持祖麻呂、車持鷹甘 *:平城宮跡出土木簡に名前が見える。 *車持若麻呂 *:平城宮跡から出土した題箋に名前が見える。
== 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}}
== 参考文献 == * 『日本書紀』（三）、[[岩波文庫]]、1994年 * 『日本書紀』（五）、岩波文庫、1995年 * [[宇治谷孟]]訳『日本書紀』全現代語訳（上）、[[講談社]]〈[[講談社学術文庫]]〉、1988年 * 宇治谷孟訳『日本書紀』全現代語訳（下）、講談社〈講談社学術文庫〉、1988年 *『続日本紀1 （新日本古典文学大系16）』 [[岩波書店]]、1989年 *『続日本紀5 （新日本古典文学大系16）』 岩波書店、1998年 * 宇治谷孟訳『続日本紀 （上）』講談社〈講談社学術文庫〉、1989年 * 宇治谷孟訳『続日本紀 （下）』講談社〈講談社学術文庫〉、1995年 * 『日本古代氏族事典』【新装版】[[佐伯有清]]：編212 - 213頁、[[雄山閣]]、2015年 * 『日本古代氏族人名辞典』275頁[[坂本太郎 (歴史学者)|坂本太郎]]・[[平野邦雄]]監修、[[吉川弘文館]]、1990年 *[[竹内理三]]・[[山田英雄 (日本史学者)|山田英雄]]・[[平野邦雄]]編『日本古代人名辞典』3 - 729頁、[[吉川弘文館]]、1972年
{{Japanese-history-stub}} {{people-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:くるまもちうし}} [[Category:車持氏|!]] [[Category:日本の氏族]] [[Category:近畿地方の氏族]] [[Category:氏]]

revision.text:
'''車持氏'''（くるまもちうじ）は「車持」を[[氏]]の名とする[[氏族]]。職業部（[[品部]]）である[[車持部]]を管掌する氏族。
== 概要 == 『[[新撰姓氏録]]』の左京皇別によると、車持公は、[[上毛野氏|上毛野朝臣]]と同じ祖先であり、[[豊城入彦命]]の8世の子孫、射狭君の末裔で、[[雄略天皇]]の時に乗與を供進したことから、「車持公」の氏姓を賜ったとある。
『[[日本書紀]]』には、[[履中天皇]]5年（推定[[404年]]）10月、天皇が急死した皇妃[[黒媛|葛城黒媛]]を埋葬した際に、神の祟りで妃を失ったことを悔やみ、その原因を探ったところ、車持君が[[筑紫国]]で調査した[[車持部]]をほしいままに検校し、さらに[[宗像神社]]に割き充てられていた車持部も奪い取ったことが発覚した。天皇は、この2つの罪状で車持君を糾弾し、[[摂津国]]長渚崎（ながすのざき）（現在の[[兵庫県]][[尼崎市]]長洲]あたり）で供え物と禊ぎをさせ、「今後、筑紫の車持部を管掌してはならない」とされたという<ref>『日本書紀』履中天皇5年10月11日条</ref>。この時に車持氏は内定氏族として、朝廷内に力を持っていたことが窺われる<ref>『日本古代氏族事典』</ref>。
『日本書紀』引用の「百済本記」によれば、[[継体天皇]]3年（[[509年]]）に「[[久羅麻致支弥]]（くらまちきみ、車持君）」が[[百済]]に遣わされ、[[任那]]の日本県（やまとのあがた）の村に逃亡してきて戸籍のなくなった百済人を、本国に送還して戸籍につけたとされる<ref>コトバンク「車持君[https://kotobank.jp/word/久羅麻致支弥-1072490]」</ref>。
[[飛鳥時代]]には、[[車持国子]]の娘の[[車持与志古娘]]が[[中臣鎌足]]に嫁ぎ[[藤原不比等]]を産んだとされる。
[[奈良時代]]には、[[車持千年]]が[[宮廷歌人]]として[[元正天皇]]と[[聖武天皇]]に仕えた。
『[[日本三代実録]]』[[元慶]]6年（[[882年]]）12月条によると、車持氏は[[律令]]体制下において[[宮内省 (律令制)|宮内省]][[主殿寮]]の[[殿部]]に属する負名人であると見え、供御の輿輦などを職務としていたとあり、『[[延喜式]]』[[践祚]][[大嘗祭]]条には、車持朝臣の1名が菅蓋を取る役目をしたことが見えている<ref>『延喜式』巻7神祇「践祚大嘗祭」40条</ref>。
同じ『三代実録』によると、元慶元年（[[877年]]）12月25日条の[[都御酉]]の賜姓記事に、崇神天皇の子孫で、上毛野氏・大野氏・池田氏・佐味氏・車持氏と同祖であるとあり、『[[続日本紀]]』[[延暦]]10年（[[791年]]）4月の[[住吉綱主|池原綱主]]の奏上によると、「池原・上毛野の2氏は[[豊城入彦命]]より出ており、彼の子孫である東国の6氏（上述5氏のほか、[[下毛野氏]]も含まれる）の朝臣は居住地によって姓を賜り、氏を称している」とある<ref>『続日本紀』延喜10年4月5日条</ref>。このことから、車持氏を含む6氏は、東国に居住し、地名を氏族名としたことが窺われ、藤原宮跡出土木簡によると、[[上野国]][[群馬郡]]は7世紀末まで「車評」であったことが判明している。『[[国内神名帳]]』の1つである『上野国神名帳』によると、「群馬郡西郡之分」の中に、「車持明神」・「車持若御子明神」の名が見え、この地が車持氏の関与する地の一つであったことが提示されている。
[[群馬県]][[高崎市]]保渡田町の[[保渡田古墳群]]には、墳丘長100メートル前後・[[舟形石棺]]の3基の[[前方後円墳]]が存在し、ともに5世紀後半から6世紀前半の築造と推定され、上毛野国形成のはじまりとなった豪族の墓と考えられている。同時期の豪族の居館跡とされる[[三ツ寺遺跡|三ツ寺Ⅰ遺跡]]も、この地域と大和政権との繋がりを示唆している。
車持氏の[[カバネ|姓]]は[[キミ (カバネ)|君]]であり、[[天武天皇]]の[[八色の姓]]で[[朝臣]]姓になっている<ref>『日本書紀』天武天皇13年11月1日条</ref>。ほかにも[[連]]・[[首 (姓)|首]]が見られる。[[藤原不比等]]の外祖父に、車持国子君がいた、という記録も見られ<ref>『尊卑分脈』</ref>、『[[正倉院文書|東南院文書]]』の「天平宝字五年（[[761年]]）十一月廿七日大和国[[十市郡]]司売買地券文解」よると、天平宝字また車持朝臣の中には、[[平城京|平城]]右京の5条2坊を本貫とするものもいた<ref>『東南院文書』三 - 五五頁</ref>。[[長岡京]]から出土された[[墨書土器]]「車宅」を、車持氏の宅であるとする説も見られる<ref>清水みき「墨書土器『車宅』をめぐって」『長岡京古文化論叢』</ref>。
== 木簡や戸籍などに見える車持氏 == *車持朝臣（名欠）、車持朝臣氏道 *:太宰府政庁遺跡から出土した木簡に名前が見える。 *車持君泥麻呂、車持君伊久沙、車持君羊 *:「豊前国仲津郡丁里大宝二年戸籍断簡」に見える、豊前国仲津郡丁里の戸主・狭度勝某の戸口。伊久沙は泥麻呂の嫡子、羊は伊久沙の弟。 *車持祖麻呂、車持鷹甘 *:平城宮跡出土木簡に名前が見える。 *車持若麻呂 *:平城宮跡から出土した題箋に名前が見える。
== 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}}
== 参考文献 == * 『日本書紀』（三）、[[岩波文庫]]、1994年 * 『日本書紀』（五）、岩波文庫、1995年 * [[宇治谷孟]]訳『日本書紀』全現代語訳（上）、[[講談社]]〈[[講談社学術文庫]]〉、1988年 * 宇治谷孟訳『日本書紀』全現代語訳（下）、講談社〈講談社学術文庫〉、1988年 *『続日本紀1 （新日本古典文学大系16）』 [[岩波書店]]、1989年 *『続日本紀5 （新日本古典文学大系16）』 岩波書店、1998年 * 宇治谷孟訳『続日本紀 （上）』講談社〈講談社学術文庫〉、1989年 * 宇治谷孟訳『続日本紀 （下）』講談社〈講談社学術文庫〉、1995年 * 『日本古代氏族事典』【新装版】[[佐伯有清]]：編212 - 213頁、[[雄山閣]]、2015年 * 『日本古代氏族人名辞典』275頁[[坂本太郎 (歴史学者)|坂本太郎]]・[[平野邦雄]]監修、[[吉川弘文館]]、1990年 *[[竹内理三]]・[[山田英雄 (日本史学者)|山田英雄]]・[[平野邦雄]]編『日本古代人名辞典』3 - 729頁、[[吉川弘文館]]、1972年

revision.comment: Created page with "'''Kuramochi Clan''' (車持氏) is a clan that takes its name, "Kuramochi," from its family lineage. This clan oversaw the occupational group ({{ill|Shinabe|ja|品部}}) known as the {{ill|Kuramochibe|ja|車持部}}, which managed specific duties. == Overview == According to the {{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|ja|新撰姓氏録}}, under the Left Capital section of imperial lineage, the Kuramochi clan shared the same ancestry as the {{ill|Kamitsukeno Clan|ja|上毛野氏}}...."
revision.text:
'''Kuramochi Clan''' (車持氏) is a clan that takes its name, "Kuramochi," from its family lineage. This clan oversaw the occupational group ({{ill|Shinabe|ja|品部}}) known as the {{ill|Kuramochibe|ja|車持部}}, which managed specific duties.
== Overview ==
According to the {{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|ja|新撰姓氏録}}, under the Left Capital section of imperial lineage, the Kuramochi clan shared the same ancestry as the {{ill|Kamitsukeno Clan|ja|上毛野氏}}. They were descendants of {{ill|Toyokiirihiko-no-mikoto|ja|豊城入彦命}}, specifically from his eighth-generation descendant Isasa no Kimi. During the reign of {{ill|Emperor Yūryaku|ja|雄略天皇}}, the clan earned the surname "Kuramochi no Kimi" after offering vehicles to the court.
The {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}} mentions an event during the 5th year of {{ill|Emperor Richū|ja|履中天皇}} (404 CE, estimated). When Emperor Richū mourned the death of his consort, {{ill|Kurohime|ja|葛城黒媛}}, divine punishment was blamed on Kuramochi no Kimi. He had misused resources assigned to {{ill|Kuramochibe|ja|車持部}} for investigations in {{ill|Tsukushi Province|ja|筑紫国}} and confiscated goods dedicated to {{ill|Munakata Shrine|ja|宗像神社}}. As a result, the emperor condemned Kuramochi no Kimi, ordering purification rites at Nagasunozaki (now Nagasu, {{ill|Amagasaki|ja|尼崎市}}), and banned him from managing Kuramochibe in Tsukushi.<ref>''Nihon Shoki,'' Emperor Richū, 5th year, 10th month, 11th day entry.</ref>
The ''Nihon Shoki,'' citing the "Baekje Annals," records that in the 3rd year of {{ill|Emperor Keitai|ja|継体天皇}} (509), Kuramochi no Kimi (久羅麻致支弥, Kuramachikimi) was sent to {{ill|Baekje|ja|百済}} to return refugees who had fled to {{ill|Mimana|ja|任那}} back to their homeland and re-register them.<ref>{{Cite web|title=久羅麻致支弥|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/久羅麻致支弥-1072490|publisher=Kotobank}}</ref>
During the {{ill|Asuka period|ja|飛鳥時代}}, Kuramochi Kunikko’s daughter, {{ill|Kuramochi Yoshikome|ja|車持与志古娘}}, married {{ill|Nakatomi no Kamatari|ja|中臣鎌足}}, giving birth to {{ill|Fujiwara no Fuhito|ja|藤原不比等}}.
In the {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}}, {{ill|Kuramochi Chitose|ja|車持千年}} served as a court poet for {{ill|Empress Genmei|ja|元明天皇}} and {{ill|Emperor Shōmu|ja|聖武天皇}}.
According to the {{ill|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|ja|日本三代実録}}, compiled in 882, the Kuramochi clan served under the {{ill|Ritsuryō|ja|律令}} system as subordinates in the {{ill|Ministry of the Imperial Household (Ritsuryō)|ja|宮内省}}’s {{ill|Shudenryō|ja|主殿寮}}. Their duties included managing imperial palanquins. The ''Engishiki'' mentions a member of the Kuramochi clan performing ceremonial roles during the {{ill|Daijōsai|ja|大嘗祭}}.<ref>''Engishiki,'' vol. 7, ''Shinji,'' "Daijōsai," Article 40.</ref>
The ''Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku'' also states that in 877, the Kuramochi clan and five other clans—Kamitsukeno, Ōno, Ikeda, Sami, and Shimotsukeno—were all descendants of Emperor Sujin. According to the ''Shoku Nihongi,'' a petition submitted by {{ill|Sumiyoshi no Tsunamori|ja|住吉綱主}} in 791 noted that these clans were settled in the eastern provinces, taking surnames based on their places of residence. Archaeological evidence from wooden tablets excavated from the Fujiwara Palace indicates that Kamitsukeno’s territory included a district named "Kuramochi District" until the late 7th century.
The {{ill|Hodota Kofun Group|ja|保渡田古墳群}} in {{ill|Takasaki|ja|高崎市}}, Gunma Prefecture, includes three keyhole-shaped burial mounds constructed between the late 5th and early 6th centuries. These are believed to be tombs of regional leaders linked to the formation of the Keno Province. The nearby {{ill|Mitsudera Site|ja|三ツ寺Ⅰ遺跡}} further highlights connections between these clans and the Yamato court.
The Kuramochi clan’s {{ill|Kabane|ja|姓}} was originally "Kimi," later elevated to "Ason" under the {{ill|Eight Prince Ranks|ja|八色の姓}} reform initiated by {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|天武天皇}}.<ref>''Nihon Shoki,'' Emperor Tenmu, 13th year, 11th month, 1st day entry.</ref>
== Kuramochi Clan in Wooden Tablets and Registers == * Kuramochi Ason (Name unknown), Kuramochi Ason Michimichi *: Names appear on wooden tablets excavated from the Dazaifu government complex. * Kuramochi no Kimi Nemaro, Kuramochi no Kimi Ikusa, Kuramochi no Kimi Hitsuji *: Appears in the fragmentary register "Bungo Province Nakatsu District Household Registry of 702." * Kuramochi no Sazumaro, Kuramochi no Takan *: Names appear on wooden tablets excavated from the Heijō Palace site. * Kuramochi no Wakamaru *: Appears on labels excavated from the Heijō Palace site.
== References == {{reflist}}
== Sources == * ''Nihon Shoki,'' Volumes 3 and 5, {{ill|Iwanami Bunko|ja|岩波文庫}}, 1994–1995. * {{ill|Ujitani Mitsuru|ja|宇治谷孟}}, ''Nihon Shoki: Complete Modern Translation,'' Vols. 1–2, {{ill|Kōdansha Gakujutsu Bunko|ja|講談社学術文庫}}, 1988. * {{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}} Volumes 1 and 5, {{ill|Iwanami Shoten|ja|岩波書店}}, 1989, 1998. * {{ill|Saeki Ariyoshi|ja|佐伯有清}}, ''Japanese Ancient Clan Encyclopedia,'' 2015. * {{ill|Sakamoto Tarō|ja|坂本太郎}}, ''Japanese Ancient Clan Names Dictionary,'' {{ill|Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|ja|吉川弘文館}}, 1990. * {{ill|Takeuchi Rizo|ja|竹内理三}}, ''Japanese Ancient Names Dictionary,'' Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1972.
{{Japanese-history-stub}} {{people-stub}}

revision.text:
'''Kuramochi Clan''' (車持氏) is a clan that takes its name, "Kuramochi," from its family lineage. This clan oversaw the occupational group ({{ill|Shinabe|ja|品部}}) known as the {{ill|Kuramochibe|ja|車持部}}, which managed specific duties.
== Overview ==
According to the {{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|ja|新撰姓氏録}}, under the Left Capital section of imperial lineage, the Kuramochi clan shared the same ancestry as the {{ill|Kamitsukeno Clan|ja|上毛野氏}}. They were descendants of {{ill|Toyokiirihiko-no-mikoto|ja|豊城入彦命}}, specifically from his eighth-generation descendant Isasa no Kimi. During the reign of {{ill|Emperor Yūryaku|ja|雄略天皇}}, the clan earned the surname "Kuramochi no Kimi" after offering vehicles to the court.
The {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}} mentions an event during the 5th year of {{ill|Emperor Richū|ja|履中天皇}} (404 CE, estimated). When Emperor Richū mourned the death of his consort, {{ill|Kurohime|ja|葛城黒媛}}, divine punishment was blamed on Kuramochi no Kimi. He had misused resources assigned to {{ill|Kuramochibe|ja|車持部}} for investigations in {{ill|Tsukushi Province|ja|筑紫国}} and confiscated goods dedicated to {{ill|Munakata Shrine|ja|宗像神社}}. As a result, the emperor condemned Kuramochi no Kimi, ordering purification rites at Nagasunozaki (now Nagasu, {{ill|Amagasaki|ja|尼崎市}}), and banned him from managing Kuramochibe in Tsukushi.<ref>''Nihon Shoki,'' Emperor Richū, 5th year, 10th month, 11th day entry.</ref>
The ''Nihon Shoki,'' citing the "Baekje Annals," records that in the 3rd year of {{ill|Emperor Keitai|ja|継体天皇}} (509), Kuramochi no Kimi (久羅麻致支弥, Kuramachikimi) was sent to {{ill|Baekje|ja|百済}} to return refugees who had fled to {{ill|Mimana|ja|任那}} back to their homeland and re-register them.<ref>{{Cite web|title=久羅麻致支弥|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/久羅麻致支弥-1072490|publisher=Kotobank}}</ref>
During the {{ill|Asuka period|ja|飛鳥時代}}, Kuramochi Kunikko’s daughter, {{ill|Kuramochi Yoshikome|ja|車持与志古娘}}, married {{ill|Nakatomi no Kamatari|ja|中臣鎌足}}, giving birth to {{ill|Fujiwara no Fuhito|ja|藤原不比等}}.
In the {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}}, {{ill|Kuramochi Chitose|ja|車持千年}} served as a court poet for {{ill|Empress Genmei|ja|元明天皇}} and {{ill|Emperor Shōmu|ja|聖武天皇}}.
According to the {{ill|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|ja|日本三代実録}}, compiled in 882, the Kuramochi clan served under the {{ill|Ritsuryō|ja|律令}} system as subordinates in the {{ill|Ministry of the Imperial Household (Ritsuryō)|ja|宮内省}}’s {{ill|Shudenryō|ja|主殿寮}}. Their duties included managing imperial palanquins. The ''Engishiki'' mentions a member of the Kuramochi clan performing ceremonial roles during the {{ill|Daijōsai|ja|大嘗祭}}.<ref>''Engishiki,'' vol. 7, ''Shinji,'' "Daijōsai," Article 40.</ref>
The ''Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku'' also states that in 877, the Kuramochi clan and five other clans—Kamitsukeno, Ōno, Ikeda, Sami, and Shimotsukeno—were all descendants of Emperor Sujin. According to the ''Shoku Nihongi,'' a petition submitted by {{ill|Sumiyoshi no Tsunamori|ja|住吉綱主}} in 791 noted that these clans were settled in the eastern provinces, taking surnames based on their places of residence. Archaeological evidence from wooden tablets excavated from the Fujiwara Palace indicates that Kamitsukeno’s territory included a district named "Kuramochi District" until the late 7th century.
The {{ill|Hodota Kofun Group|ja|保渡田古墳群}} in {{ill|Takasaki|ja|高崎市}}, Gunma Prefecture, includes three keyhole-shaped burial mounds constructed between the late 5th and early 6th centuries. These are believed to be tombs of regional leaders linked to the formation of the Keno Province. The nearby {{ill|Mitsudera Site|ja|三ツ寺Ⅰ遺跡}} further highlights connections between these clans and the Yamato court.
The Kuramochi clan’s {{ill|Kabane|ja|姓}} was originally "Kimi," later elevated to "Ason" under the {{ill|Eight Prince Ranks|ja|八色の姓}} reform initiated by {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|天武天皇}}.<ref>''Nihon Shoki,'' Emperor Tenmu, 13th year, 11th month, 1st day entry.</ref>
== Kuramochi Clan in Wooden Tablets and Registers == * Kuramochi Ason (Name unknown), Kuramochi Ason Michimichi *: Names appear on wooden tablets excavated from the Dazaifu government complex. * Kuramochi no Kimi Nemaro, Kuramochi no Kimi Ikusa, Kuramochi no Kimi Hitsuji *: Appears in the fragmentary register "Bungo Province Nakatsu District Household Registry of 702." * Kuramochi no Sazumaro, Kuramochi no Takan *: Names appear on wooden tablets excavated from the Heijō Palace site. * Kuramochi no Wakamaru *: Appears on labels excavated from the Heijō Palace site.
== References == {{reflist}}
== Sources == * ''Nihon Shoki,'' Volumes 3 and 5, {{ill|Iwanami Bunko|ja|岩波文庫}}, 1994–1995. * {{ill|Ujitani Mitsuru|ja|宇治谷孟}}, ''Nihon Shoki: Complete Modern Translation,'' Vols. 1–2, {{ill|Kōdansha Gakujutsu Bunko|ja|講談社学術文庫}}, 1988. * {{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}} Volumes 1 and 5, {{ill|Iwanami Shoten|ja|岩波書店}}, 1989, 1998. * {{ill|Saeki Ariyoshi|ja|佐伯有清}}, ''Japanese Ancient Clan Encyclopedia,'' 2015. * {{ill|Sakamoto Tarō|ja|坂本太郎}}, ''Japanese Ancient Clan Names Dictionary,'' {{ill|Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|ja|吉川弘文館}}, 1990. * {{ill|Takeuchi Rizo|ja|竹内理三}}, ''Japanese Ancient Names Dictionary,'' Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1972.

[[ja:車持氏]]

'''Kuramochi Clan''' (車持氏) is a clan that takes its name, "Kuramochi," from its family lineage. This clan oversaw the occupational group ({{ill|Shinabe|ja|品部}}) known as the {{ill|Kuramochibe|ja|車持部}}, which managed specific duties.
== Overview ==
According to the {{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|ja|新撰姓氏録}}, under the Left Capital section of imperial lineage, the Kuramochi clan shared the same ancestry as the {{ill|Kamitsukeno clan|ja|上毛野氏}}. They were descendants of {{ill|Toyokiirihiko-no-mikoto|ja|豊城入彦命}}, specifically from his eighth-generation descendant Isasa no Kimi. During the reign of {{ill|Emperor Yūryaku|ja|雄略天皇}}, the clan earned the surname "Kuramochi no Kimi" after offering vehicles to the court.
The {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}} mentions an event during the 5th year of {{ill|Emperor Richū|ja|履中天皇}} (404 CE, estimated). When Emperor Richū mourned the death of his consort, {{ill|Kurohime|ja|葛城黒媛}}, divine punishment was blamed on Kuramochi no Kimi. He had misused resources assigned to {{ill|Kuramochibe|ja|車持部}} for investigations in {{ill|Tsukushi Province|ja|筑紫国}} and confiscated goods dedicated to {{ill|Munakata Shrine|ja|宗像神社}}. As a result, the emperor condemned Kuramochi no Kimi, ordering purification rites at Nagasunozaki (now Nagasu, {{ill|Amagasaki|ja|尼崎市}}), and banned him from managing Kuramochibe in Tsukushi.<ref>''Nihon Shoki,'' Emperor Richū, 5th year, 10th month, 11th day entry.</ref>
The ''Nihon Shoki,'' citing the "Baekje Annals," records that in the 3rd year of {{ill|Emperor Keitai|ja|継体天皇}} (509), Kuramochi no Kimi (久羅麻致支弥, Kuramachikimi) was sent to {{ill|Baekje|ja|百済}} to return refugees who had fled to {{ill|Mimana|ja|任那}} back to their homeland and re-register them.<ref>{{Cite web|title=久羅麻致支弥|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/久羅麻致支弥-1072490|publisher=Kotobank}}</ref>
During the {{ill|Asuka period|ja|飛鳥時代}}, Kuramochi Kunikko’s daughter, {{ill|Kuramochi Yoshikome|ja|車持与志古娘}}, married {{ill|Nakatomi no Kamatari|ja|中臣鎌足}}, giving birth to {{ill|Fujiwara no Fuhito|ja|藤原不比等}}.
In the {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}}, {{ill|Kuramochi Chitose|ja|車持千年}} served as a court poet for {{ill|Empress Genmei|ja|元明天皇}} and {{ill|Emperor Shōmu|ja|聖武天皇}}.
According to the {{ill|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|ja|日本三代実録}}, compiled in 882, the Kuramochi clan served under the {{ill|Ritsuryō|ja|律令}} system as subordinates in the {{ill|Ministry of the Imperial Household (Ritsuryō)|ja|宮内省}}’s {{ill|Shudenryō|ja|主殿寮}}. Their duties included managing imperial palanquins. The ''Engishiki'' mentions a member of the Kuramochi clan performing ceremonial roles during the {{ill|Daijōsai|ja|大嘗祭}}.<ref>''Engishiki,'' vol. 7, ''Shinji,'' "Daijōsai," Article 40.</ref>
The ''Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku'' also states that in 877, the Kuramochi clan and five other clans—Kamitsukeno, Ōno, Ikeda, Sami, and Shimotsukeno—were all descendants of Emperor Sujin. According to the ''Shoku Nihongi,'' a petition submitted by {{ill|Sumiyoshi no Tsunamori|ja|住吉綱主}} in 791 noted that these clans were settled in the eastern provinces, taking surnames based on their places of residence. Archaeological evidence from wooden tablets excavated from the Fujiwara Palace indicates that Kamitsukeno’s territory included a district named "Kuramochi District" until the late 7th century.
The {{ill|Hodota Kofun Group|ja|保渡田古墳群}} in {{ill|Takasaki|ja|高崎市}}, Gunma Prefecture, includes three keyhole-shaped burial mounds constructed between the late 5th and early 6th centuries. These are believed to be tombs of regional leaders linked to the formation of the Keno Province. The nearby {{ill|Mitsudera Site|ja|三ツ寺Ⅰ遺跡}} further highlights connections between these clans and the Yamato court.
The Kuramochi clan’s {{ill|Kabane|ja|姓}} was originally "Kimi," later elevated to "Ason" under the {{ill|Eight Prince Ranks|ja|八色の姓}} reform initiated by {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|天武天皇}}.<ref>''Nihon Shoki,'' Emperor Tenmu, 13th year, 11th month, 1st day entry.</ref>
== Kuramochi Clan in Wooden Tablets and Registers == * Kuramochi Ason (Name unknown), Kuramochi Ason Michimichi *: Names appear on wooden tablets excavated from the Dazaifu government complex. * Kuramochi no Kimi Nemaro, Kuramochi no Kimi Ikusa, Kuramochi no Kimi Hitsuji *: Appears in the fragmentary register "Bungo Province Nakatsu District Household Registry of 702." * Kuramochi no Sazumaro, Kuramochi no Takan *: Names appear on wooden tablets excavated from the Heijō Palace site. * Kuramochi no Wakamaru *: Appears on labels excavated from the Heijō Palace site.
== References == {{reflist}}
== Sources == * ''Nihon Shoki,'' Volumes 3 and 5, {{ill|Iwanami Bunko|ja|岩波文庫}}, 1994–1995. * {{ill|Ujitani Mitsuru|ja|宇治谷孟}}, ''Nihon Shoki: Complete Modern Translation,'' Vols. 1–2, {{ill|Kōdansha Gakujutsu Bunko|ja|講談社学術文庫}}, 1988. * {{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}} Volumes 1 and 5, {{ill|Iwanami Shoten|ja|岩波書店}}, 1989, 1998. * {{ill|Saeki Ariyoshi|ja|佐伯有清}}, ''Japanese Ancient Clan Encyclopedia,'' 2015. * {{ill|Sakamoto Tarō|ja|坂本太郎}}, ''Japanese Ancient Clan Names Dictionary,'' {{ill|Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|ja|吉川弘文館}}, 1990. * {{ill|Takeuchi Rizo|ja|竹内理三}}, ''Japanese Ancient Names Dictionary,'' Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1972.
{{Japanese-history-stub}} {{people-stub}}

== Kuramochi Clan in Wooden Tablets and Registers == * Kuramochi Ason (Name unknown), Kuramochi Ason Michimichi *: Names appear on wooden tablets excavated from the Dazaifu government complex. * Kuramochi no Kimi Nemaro, Kuramochi no Kimi Ikusa, Kuramochi no Kimi Hitsuji *: Appears in the fragmentary register "Bungo Province Nakatsu District Household Registry of 702." * Kuramochi no Sazumaro, Kuramochi no Takan *: Names appear on wooden tablets excavated from the Heijō Palace site. * Kuramochi no Wakamaru *: Appears on labels excavated from the Heijō Palace site.
== References == {{reflist}}
== Sources == * ''Nihon Shoki,'' Volumes 3 and 5, {{ill|Iwanami Bunko|ja|岩波文庫}}, 1994–1995. * {{ill|Ujitani Mitsuru|ja|宇治谷孟}}, ''Nihon Shoki: Complete Modern Translation,'' Vols. 1–2, {{ill|Kōdansha Gakujutsu Bunko|ja|講談社学術文庫}}, 1988. * {{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}} Volumes 1 and 5, {{ill|Iwanami Shoten|ja|岩波書店}}, 1989, 1998. * {{ill|Saeki Ariyoshi|ja|佐伯有清}}, ''Japanese Ancient Clan Encyclopedia,'' 2015. * {{ill|Sakamoto Tarō|ja|坂本太郎}}, ''Japanese Ancient Clan Names Dictionary,'' {{ill|Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|ja|吉川弘文館}}, 1990. * {{ill|Takeuchi Rizo|ja|竹内理三}}, ''Japanese Ancient Names Dictionary,'' Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1972.

[[Category:dd]] [[Category:bump]] [[ja:車持氏]]

'''Kuramochi Clan''' (車持氏) is a clan that takes its name, "Kuramochi," from its family lineage. This clan oversaw the occupational group ({{ill|Shinabe|ja|品部}}) known as the {{ill|Kuramochibe|ja|車持部}}, which managed specific duties.
== Overview ==
According to the {{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|ja|新撰姓氏録}}, under the Left Capital section of imperial lineage, the Kuramochi clan shared the same ancestry as the {{ill|Kamitsukeno clan|ja|上毛野氏}}. They were descendants of {{ill|Toyokiirihiko-no-mikoto|ja|豊城入彦命}}, specifically from his eighth-generation descendant Isasa no Kimi. During the reign of {{ill|Emperor Yūryaku|ja|雄略天皇}}, the clan earned the surname "Kuramochi no Kimi" after offering vehicles to the court.
The {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}} mentions an event during the 5th year of {{ill|Emperor Richū|ja|履中天皇}} (404 CE, estimated). When Emperor Richū mourned the death of his consort, {{ill|Kurohime|ja|葛城黒媛}}, divine punishment was blamed on Kuramochi no Kimi. He had misused resources assigned to {{ill|Kuramochibe|ja|車持部}} for investigations in {{ill|Tsukushi Province|ja|筑紫国}} and confiscated goods dedicated to {{ill|Munakata Shrine|ja|宗像神社}}. As a result, the emperor condemned Kuramochi no Kimi, ordering purification rites at Nagasunozaki (now Nagasu, {{ill|Amagasaki|ja|尼崎市}}), and banned him from managing Kuramochibe in Tsukushi.<ref>''Nihon Shoki,'' Emperor Richū, 5th year, 10th month, 11th day entry.</ref>
The ''Nihon Shoki,'' citing the "Baekje Annals," records that in the 3rd year of {{ill|Emperor Keitai|ja|継体天皇}} (509), Kuramochi no Kimi (久羅麻致支弥, Kuramachikimi) was sent to {{ill|Baekje|ja|百済}} to return refugees who had fled to {{ill|Mimana|ja|任那}} back to their homeland and re-register them.<ref>{{Cite web|title=久羅麻致支弥|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/久羅麻致支弥-1072490|publisher=Kotobank}}</ref>
During the {{ill|Asuka period|ja|飛鳥時代}}, Kuramochi Kunikko’s daughter, {{ill|Kuramochi Yoshikome|ja|車持与志古娘}}, married {{ill|Nakatomi no Kamatari|ja|中臣鎌足}}, giving birth to {{ill|Fujiwara no Fuhito|ja|藤原不比等}}.
In the {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}}, {{ill|Kuramochi Chitose|ja|車持千年}} served as a court poet for {{ill|Empress Genmei|ja|元明天皇}} and {{ill|Emperor Shōmu|ja|聖武天皇}}.
According to the {{ill|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|ja|日本三代実録}}, compiled in 882, the Kuramochi clan served under the {{ill|Ritsuryō|ja|律令}} system as subordinates in the {{ill|Ministry of the Imperial Household (Ritsuryō)|ja|宮内省}}’s {{ill|Shudenryō|ja|主殿寮}}. Their duties included managing imperial palanquins. The ''Engishiki'' mentions a member of the Kuramochi clan performing ceremonial roles during the {{ill|Daijōsai|ja|大嘗祭}}.<ref>''Engishiki,'' vol. 7, ''Shinji,'' "Daijōsai," Article 40.</ref>
The ''Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku'' also states that in 877, the Kuramochi clan and five other clans—Kamitsukeno, Ōno, Ikeda, Sami, and Shimotsukeno—were all descendants of Emperor Sujin. According to the ''Shoku Nihongi,'' a petition submitted by {{ill|Sumiyoshi no Tsunamori|ja|住吉綱主}} in 791 noted that these clans were settled in the eastern provinces, taking surnames based on their places of residence. Archaeological evidence from wooden tablets excavated from the Fujiwara Palace indicates that Kamitsukeno’s territory included a district named "Kuramochi District" until the late 7th century.
The {{ill|Hodota Kofun Group|ja|保渡田古墳群}} in {{ill|Takasaki|ja|高崎市}}, Gunma Prefecture, includes three keyhole-shaped burial mounds constructed between the late 5th and early 6th centuries. These are believed to be tombs of regional leaders linked to the formation of the Keno Province. The nearby {{ill|Mitsudera Site|ja|三ツ寺Ⅰ遺跡}} further highlights connections between these clans and the Yamato court.
The Kuramochi clan’s {{ill|Kabane|ja|姓}} was originally "Kimi," later elevated to "Ason" under the {{ill|Eight Prince Ranks|ja|八色の姓}} reform initiated by {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|天武天皇}}.<ref>''Nihon Shoki,'' Emperor Tenmu, 13th year, 11th month, 1st day entry.</ref>
== Kuramochi Clan in Wooden Tablets and Registers == * Kuramochi Ason (Name unknown), Kuramochi Ason Michimichi *: Names appear on wooden tablets excavated from the Dazaifu government complex. * Kuramochi no Kimi Nemaro, Kuramochi no Kimi Ikusa, Kuramochi no Kimi Hitsuji *: Appears in the fragmentary register "Bungo Province Nakatsu District Household Registry of 702." * Kuramochi no Sazumaro, Kuramochi no Takan *: Names appear on wooden tablets excavated from the Heijō Palace site. * Kuramochi no Wakamaru *: Appears on labels excavated from the Heijō Palace site.
== References == {{reflist}}
== Sources == * ''Nihon Shoki,'' Volumes 3 and 5, {{ill|Iwanami Bunko|ja|岩波文庫}}, 1994–1995. * {{ill|Ujitani Mitsuru|ja|宇治谷孟}}, ''Nihon Shoki: Complete Modern Translation,'' Vols. 1–2, {{ill|Kōdansha Gakujutsu Bunko|ja|講談社学術文庫}}, 1988. * {{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}} Volumes 1 and 5, {{ill|Iwanami Shoten|ja|岩波書店}}, 1989, 1998. * {{ill|Saeki Ariyoshi|ja|佐伯有清}}, ''Japanese Ancient Clan Encyclopedia,'' 2015. * {{ill|Sakamoto Tarō|ja|坂本太郎}}, ''Japanese Ancient Clan Names Dictionary,'' {{ill|Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|ja|吉川弘文館}}, 1990. * {{ill|Takeuchi Rizo|ja|竹内理三}}, ''Japanese Ancient Names Dictionary,'' Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1972.
{{Japanese-history-stub}} {{people-stub}}

During the {{ill|Asuka period|ja|飛鳥時代}}, Kuramochi Kunikko’s daughter, {{ill|Kuramochi Yoshikome|ja|車持与志古娘}}, married {{ill|Nakatomi no Kamatari|ja|中臣鎌足}}, giving birth to {{ill|Fujiwara no Fuhito|ja|藤原不比等}}.
In the {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}}, {{ill|Kuramochi Chitose|ja|車持千年}} served as a court poet for {{ill|Empress Genmei|ja|元明天皇}} and {{ill|Emperor Shōmu|ja|聖武天皇}}.
According to the {{ill|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|ja|日本三代実録}}, compiled in 882, the Kuramochi clan served under the {{ill|Ritsuryō|ja|律令}} system as subordinates in the {{ill|Ministry of the Imperial Household (Ritsuryō)|ja|宮内省}}’s {{ill|Shudenryō|ja|主殿寮}}. Their duties included managing imperial palanquins. The ''Engishiki'' mentions a member of the Kuramochi clan performing ceremonial roles during the {{ill|Daijōsai|ja|大嘗祭}}.<ref>''Engishiki,'' vol. 7, ''Shinji,'' "Daijōsai," Article 40.</ref>
The ''Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku'' also states that in 877, the Kuramochi clan and five other clans—Kamitsukeno, Ōno, Ikeda, Sami, and Shimotsukeno—were all descendants of Emperor Sujin. According to the ''Shoku Nihongi,'' a petition submitted by {{ill|Sumiyoshi no Tsunamori|ja|住吉綱主}} in 791 noted that these clans were settled in the eastern provinces, taking surnames based on their places of residence. Archaeological evidence from wooden tablets excavated from the Fujiwara Palace indicates that Kamitsukeno’s territory included a district named "Kuramochi District" until the late 7th century.
The {{ill|Hodota Kofun Group|ja|保渡田古墳群}} in {{ill|Takasaki|ja|高崎市}}, Gunma Prefecture, includes three keyhole-shaped burial mounds constructed between the late 5th and early 6th centuries. These are believed to be tombs of regional leaders linked to the formation of the Keno Province. The nearby {{ill|Mitsudera Site|ja|三ツ寺Ⅰ遺跡}} further highlights connections between these clans and the Yamato court.
The Kuramochi clan’s {{ill|Kabane|ja|姓}} was originally "Kimi," later elevated to "Ason" under the {{ill|Eight Prince Ranks|ja|八色の姓}} reform initiated by {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|天武天皇}}.<ref>''Nihon Shoki,'' Emperor Tenmu, 13th year, 11th month, 1st day entry.</ref>
== Kuramochi Clan in Wooden Tablets and Registers == * Kuramochi Ason (Name unknown), Kuramochi Ason Michimichi *: Names appear on wooden tablets excavated from the Dazaifu government complex. * Kuramochi no Kimi Nemaro, Kuramochi no Kimi Ikusa, Kuramochi no Kimi Hitsuji *: Appears in the fragmentary register "Bungo Province Nakatsu District Household Registry of 702." * Kuramochi no Sazumaro, Kuramochi no Takan *: Names appear on wooden tablets excavated from the Heijō Palace site. * Kuramochi no Wakamaru *: Appears on labels excavated from the Heijō Palace site.
== References == {{reflist}}
== Sources == * ''Nihon Shoki,'' Volumes 3 and 5, {{ill|Iwanami Bunko|ja|岩波文庫}}, 1994–1995. * {{ill|Ujitani Mitsuru|ja|宇治谷孟}}, ''Nihon Shoki: Complete Modern Translation,'' Vols. 1–2, {{ill|Kōdansha Gakujutsu Bunko|ja|講談社学術文庫}}, 1988. * {{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}} Volumes 1 and 5, {{ill|Iwanami Shoten|ja|岩波書店}}, 1989, 1998. * {{ill|Saeki Ariyoshi|ja|佐伯有清}}, ''Japanese Ancient Clan Encyclopedia,'' 2015. * {{ill|Sakamoto Tarō|ja|坂本太郎}}, ''Japanese Ancient Clan Names Dictionary,'' {{ill|Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|ja|吉川弘文館}}, 1990. * {{ill|Takeuchi Rizo|ja|竹内理三}}, ''Japanese Ancient Names Dictionary,'' Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1972.

revision.text:
'''Kuramochi Clan''' (車持氏) is a clan that takes its name, "Kuramochi," from its family lineage. This clan oversaw the occupational group ({{ill|Shinabe|ja|品部}}) known as the {{ill|Kuramochibe|ja|車持部}}, which managed specific duties.
== Overview ==
According to the {{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|ja|新撰姓氏録}}, under the Left Capital section of imperial lineage, the Kuramochi clan shared the same ancestry as the {{ill|Kamitsukeno clan|ja|上毛野氏}}. They were descendants of {{ill|Toyokiirihiko-no-mikoto|ja|豊城入彦命}}, specifically from his eighth-generation descendant Isasa no Kimi. During the reign of {{ill|Emperor Yūryaku|ja|雄略天皇}}, the clan earned the surname "Kuramochi no Kimi" after offering vehicles to the court.
The {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}} mentions an event during the 5th year of {{ill|Emperor Richū|ja|履中天皇}} (404 CE, estimated). When Emperor Richū mourned the death of his consort, {{ill|Kurohime|ja|葛城黒媛}}, divine punishment was blamed on Kuramochi no Kimi. He had misused resources assigned to {{ill|Kuramochibe|ja|車持部}} for investigations in {{ill|Tsukushi Province|ja|筑紫国}} and confiscated goods dedicated to {{ill|Munakata Shrine|ja|宗像神社}}. As a result, the emperor condemned Kuramochi no Kimi, ordering purification rites at Nagasunozaki (now Nagasu, {{ill|Amagasaki|ja|尼崎市}}), and banned him from managing Kuramochibe in Tsukushi.<ref>''Nihon Shoki,'' Emperor Richū, 5th year, 10th month, 11th day entry.</ref>
The ''Nihon Shoki,'' citing the "Baekje Annals," records that in the 3rd year of {{ill|Emperor Keitai|ja|継体天皇}} (509), Kuramochi no Kimi (久羅麻致支弥, Kuramachikimi) was sent to {{ill|Baekje|ja|百済}} to return refugees who had fled to {{ill|Mimana|ja|任那}} back to their homeland and re-register them.<ref>{{Cite web|title=久羅麻致支弥|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/久羅麻致支弥-1072490|publisher=Kotobank}}</ref>
During the {{ill|Asuka period|ja|飛鳥時代}}, Kuramochi Kunikko’s daughter, {{ill|Kuramochi Yoshikome|ja|車持与志古娘}}, married {{ill|Nakatomi no Kamatari|ja|中臣鎌足}}, giving birth to {{ill|Fujiwara no Fuhito|ja|藤原不比等}}.
In the {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}}, {{ill|Kuramochi Chitose|ja|車持千年}} served as a court poet for {{ill|Empress Genmei|ja|元明天皇}} and {{ill|Emperor Shōmu|ja|聖武天皇}}.
According to the {{ill|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|ja|日本三代実録}}, compiled in 882, the Kuramochi clan served under the {{ill|Ritsuryō|ja|律令}} system as subordinates in the {{ill|Ministry of the Imperial Household (Ritsuryō)|ja|宮内省}}’s {{ill|Shudenryō|ja|主殿寮}}. Their duties included managing imperial palanquins. The ''Engishiki'' mentions a member of the Kuramochi clan performing ceremonial roles during the {{ill|Daijōsai|ja|大嘗祭}}.<ref>''Engishiki,'' vol. 7, ''Shinji,'' "Daijōsai," Article 40.</ref>
The ''Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku'' also states that in 877, the Kuramochi clan and five other clans—Kamitsukeno, Ōno, Ikeda, Sami, and Shimotsukeno—were all descendants of Emperor Sujin. According to the ''Shoku Nihongi,'' a petition submitted by {{ill|Sumiyoshi no Tsunamori|ja|住吉綱主}} in 791 noted that these clans were settled in the eastern provinces, taking surnames based on their places of residence. Archaeological evidence from wooden tablets excavated from the Fujiwara Palace indicates that Kamitsukeno’s territory included a district named "Kuramochi District" until the late 7th century.
The {{ill|Hodota Kofun Group|ja|保渡田古墳群}} in {{ill|Takasaki|ja|高崎市}}, Gunma Prefecture, includes three keyhole-shaped burial mounds constructed between the late 5th and early 6th centuries. These are believed to be tombs of regional leaders linked to the formation of the Keno Province. The nearby {{ill|Mitsudera Site|ja|三ツ寺Ⅰ遺跡}} further highlights connections between these clans and the Yamato court.
The Kuramochi clan’s {{ill|Kabane|ja|姓}} was originally "Kimi," later elevated to "Ason" under the {{ill|Eight Prince Ranks|ja|八色の姓}} reform initiated by {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|天武天皇}}.<ref>''Nihon Shoki,'' Emperor Tenmu, 13th year, 11th month, 1st day entry.</ref>
== Kuramochi Clan in Wooden Tablets and Registers == * Kuramochi Ason (Name unknown), Kuramochi Ason Michimichi *: Names appear on wooden tablets excavated from the Dazaifu government complex. * Kuramochi no Kimi Nemaro, Kuramochi no Kimi Ikusa, Kuramochi no Kimi Hitsuji *: Appears in the fragmentary register "Bungo Province Nakatsu District Household Registry of 702." * Kuramochi no Sazumaro, Kuramochi no Takan *: Names appear on wooden tablets excavated from the Heijō Palace site. * Kuramochi no Wakamaru *: Appears on labels excavated from the Heijō Palace site.
== References == {{reflist}}
== Sources == * ''Nihon Shoki,'' Volumes 3 and 5, {{ill|Iwanami Bunko|ja|岩波文庫}}, 1994–1995. * {{ill|Ujitani Mitsuru|ja|宇治谷孟}}, ''Nihon Shoki: Complete Modern Translation,'' Vols. 1–2, {{ill|Kōdansha Gakujutsu Bunko|ja|講談社学術文庫}}, 1988. * {{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}} Volumes 1 and 5, {{ill|Iwanami Shoten|ja|岩波書店}}, 1989, 1998. * {{ill|Saeki Ariyoshi|ja|佐伯有清}}, ''Japanese Ancient Clan Encyclopedia,'' 2015. * {{ill|Sakamoto Tarō|ja|坂本太郎}}, ''Japanese Ancient Clan Names Dictionary,'' {{ill|Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|ja|吉川弘文館}}, 1990. * {{ill|Takeuchi Rizo|ja|竹内理三}}, ''Japanese Ancient Names Dictionary,'' Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1972.

[[Category:unsorted link categories]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:unsorted link categories]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:unsorted link categories]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Kuramochi clan]] [[Category:unsorted link categories]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:unsorted link categories]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:unsorted link categories]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Japanese clans]] [[Category:unsorted link categories]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:unsorted link categories]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:unsorted link categories]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Kinki region clans]] [[Category:unsorted link categories]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:unsorted link categories]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:unsorted link categories]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Clans]] [[Category:unsorted link categories]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:unsorted link categories]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:bump]]
[[ja:車持氏]]

'''Kuramochi Clan''' (車持氏) is a clan that takes its name, "Kuramochi," from its family lineage. This clan oversaw the occupational group ({{ill|Shinabe|ja|品部}}) known as the {{ill|Kuramochibe|ja|車持部}}, which managed specific duties.
== Overview ==
According to the {{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|ja|新撰姓氏録}}, under the Left Capital section of imperial lineage, the Kuramochi clan shared the same ancestry as the {{ill|Kamitsukeno clan|ja|上毛野氏}}. They were descendants of {{ill|Toyokiirihiko-no-mikoto|ja|豊城入彦命}}, specifically from his eighth-generation descendant Isasa no Kimi. During the reign of {{ill|Emperor Yūryaku|ja|雄略天皇}}, the clan earned the surname "Kuramochi no Kimi" after offering vehicles to the court.
The {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}} mentions an event during the 5th year of {{ill|Emperor Richū|ja|履中天皇}} (404 CE, estimated). When Emperor Richū mourned the death of his consort, {{ill|Kurohime|ja|葛城黒媛}}, divine punishment was blamed on Kuramochi no Kimi. He had misused resources assigned to {{ill|Kuramochibe|ja|車持部}} for investigations in {{ill|Tsukushi Province|ja|筑紫国}} and confiscated goods dedicated to {{ill|Munakata Shrine|ja|宗像神社}}. As a result, the emperor condemned Kuramochi no Kimi, ordering purification rites at Nagasunozaki (now Nagasu, {{ill|Amagasaki|ja|尼崎市}}), and banned him from managing Kuramochibe in Tsukushi.<ref>''Nihon Shoki,'' Emperor Richū, 5th year, 10th month, 11th day entry.</ref>
The ''Nihon Shoki,'' citing the "Baekje Annals," records that in the 3rd year of {{ill|Emperor Keitai|ja|継体天皇}} (509), Kuramochi no Kimi (久羅麻致支弥, Kuramachikimi) was sent to {{ill|Baekje|ja|百済}} to return refugees who had fled to {{ill|Mimana|ja|任那}} back to their homeland and re-register them.<ref>{{Cite web|title=久羅麻致支弥|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/久羅麻致支弥-1072490|publisher=Kotobank}}</ref>
During the {{ill|Asuka period|ja|飛鳥時代}}, Kuramochi Kunikko’s daughter, {{ill|Kuramochi Yoshikome|ja|車持与志古娘}}, married {{ill|Nakatomi no Kamatari|ja|中臣鎌足}}, giving birth to {{ill|Fujiwara no Fuhito|ja|藤原不比等}}.
In the {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}}, {{ill|Kuramochi Chitose|ja|車持千年}} served as a court poet for {{ill|Empress Genmei|ja|元明天皇}} and {{ill|Emperor Shōmu|ja|聖武天皇}}.
According to the {{ill|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|ja|日本三代実録}}, compiled in 882, the Kuramochi clan served under the {{ill|Ritsuryō|ja|律令}} system as subordinates in the {{ill|Ministry of the Imperial Household (Ritsuryō)|ja|宮内省}}’s {{ill|Shudenryō|ja|主殿寮}}. Their duties included managing imperial palanquins. The ''Engishiki'' mentions a member of the Kuramochi clan performing ceremonial roles during the {{ill|Daijōsai|ja|大嘗祭}}.<ref>''Engishiki,'' vol. 7, ''Shinji,'' "Daijōsai," Article 40.</ref>
The ''Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku'' also states that in 877, the Kuramochi clan and five other clans—Kamitsukeno, Ōno, Ikeda, Sami, and Shimotsukeno—were all descendants of Emperor Sujin. According to the ''Shoku Nihongi,'' a petition submitted by {{ill|Sumiyoshi no Tsunamori|ja|住吉綱主}} in 791 noted that these clans were settled in the eastern provinces, taking surnames based on their places of residence. Archaeological evidence from wooden tablets excavated from the Fujiwara Palace indicates that Kamitsukeno’s territory included a district named "Kuramochi District" until the late 7th century.
The {{ill|Hodota Kofun Group|ja|保渡田古墳群}} in {{ill|Takasaki|ja|高崎市}}, Gunma Prefecture, includes three keyhole-shaped burial mounds constructed between the late 5th and early 6th centuries. These are believed to be tombs of regional leaders linked to the formation of the Keno Province. The nearby {{ill|Mitsudera Site|ja|三ツ寺Ⅰ遺跡}} further highlights connections between these clans and the Yamato court.
The Kuramochi clan’s {{ill|Kabane|ja|姓}} was originally "Kimi," later elevated to "Ason" under the {{ill|Eight Prince Ranks|ja|八色の姓}} reform initiated by {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|天武天皇}}.<ref>''Nihon Shoki,'' Emperor Tenmu, 13th year, 11th month, 1st day entry.</ref>
== Kuramochi Clan in Wooden Tablets and Registers == * Kuramochi Ason (Name unknown), Kuramochi Ason Michimichi *: Names appear on wooden tablets excavated from the Dazaifu government complex. * Kuramochi no Kimi Nemaro, Kuramochi no Kimi Ikusa, Kuramochi no Kimi Hitsuji *: Appears in the fragmentary register "Bungo Province Nakatsu District Household Registry of 702." * Kuramochi no Sazumaro, Kuramochi no Takan *: Names appear on wooden tablets excavated from the Heijō Palace site. * Kuramochi no Wakamaru *: Appears on labels excavated from the Heijō Palace site.
== References == {{reflist}}
== Sources == * ''Nihon Shoki,'' Volumes 3 and 5, {{ill|Iwanami Bunko|ja|岩波文庫}}, 1994–1995. * {{ill|Ujitani Mitsuru|ja|宇治谷孟}}, ''Nihon Shoki: Complete Modern Translation,'' Vols. 1–2, {{ill|Kōdansha Gakujutsu Bunko|ja|講談社学術文庫}}, 1988. * {{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}} Volumes 1 and 5, {{ill|Iwanami Shoten|ja|岩波書店}}, 1989, 1998. * {{ill|Saeki Ariyoshi|ja|佐伯有清}}, ''Japanese Ancient Clan Encyclopedia,'' 2015. * {{ill|Sakamoto Tarō|ja|坂本太郎}}, ''Japanese Ancient Clan Names Dictionary,'' {{ill|Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|ja|吉川弘文館}}, 1990. * {{ill|Takeuchi Rizo|ja|竹内理三}}, ''Japanese Ancient Names Dictionary,'' Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1972.

During the {{ill|Asuka period|ja|飛鳥時代}}, Kuramochi Kunikko’s daughter, {{ill|Kuramochi Yoshikome|ja|車持与志古娘}}, married {{ill|Nakatomi no Kamatari|ja|中臣鎌足}}, giving birth to {{ill|Fujiwara no Fuhito|ja|藤原不比等}}.
In the {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}}, {{ill|Kuramochi Chitose|ja|車持千年}} served as a court poet for {{ill|Empress Genmei|ja|元明天皇}} and {{ill|Emperor Shōmu|ja|聖武天皇}}.
According to the {{ill|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|ja|日本三代実録}}, compiled in 882, the Kuramochi clan served under the {{ill|Ritsuryō|ja|律令}} system as subordinates in the {{ill|Ministry of the Imperial Household (Ritsuryō)|ja|宮内省}}’s {{ill|Shudenryō|ja|主殿寮}}. Their duties included managing imperial palanquins. The ''Engishiki'' mentions a member of the Kuramochi clan performing ceremonial roles during the {{ill|Daijōsai|ja|大嘗祭}}.<ref>''Engishiki,'' vol. 7, ''Shinji,'' "Daijōsai," Article 40.</ref>
The ''Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku'' also states that in 877, the Kuramochi clan and five other clans—Kamitsukeno, Ōno, Ikeda, Sami, and Shimotsukeno—were all descendants of Emperor Sujin. According to the ''Shoku Nihongi,'' a petition submitted by {{ill|Sumiyoshi no Tsunamori|ja|住吉綱主}} in 791 noted that these clans were settled in the eastern provinces, taking surnames based on their places of residence. Archaeological evidence from wooden tablets excavated from the Fujiwara Palace indicates that Kamitsukeno’s territory included a district named "Kuramochi District" until the late 7th century.
The {{ill|Hodota Kofun Group|ja|保渡田古墳群}} in {{ill|Takasaki|ja|高崎市}}, Gunma Prefecture, includes three keyhole-shaped burial mounds constructed between the late 5th and early 6th centuries. These are believed to be tombs of regional leaders linked to the formation of the Keno Province. The nearby {{ill|Mitsudera Site|ja|三ツ寺Ⅰ遺跡}} further highlights connections between these clans and the Yamato court.
The Kuramochi clan’s {{ill|Kabane|ja|姓}} was originally "Kimi," later elevated to "Ason" under the {{ill|Eight Prince Ranks|ja|八色の姓}} reform initiated by {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|天武天皇}}.<ref>''Nihon Shoki,'' Emperor Tenmu, 13th year, 11th month, 1st day entry.</ref>
== Kuramochi Clan in Wooden Tablets and Registers == * Kuramochi Ason (Name unknown), Kuramochi Ason Michimichi *: Names appear on wooden tablets excavated from the Dazaifu government complex. * Kuramochi no Kimi Nemaro, Kuramochi no Kimi Ikusa, Kuramochi no Kimi Hitsuji *: Appears in the fragmentary register "Bungo Province Nakatsu District Household Registry of 702." * Kuramochi no Sazumaro, Kuramochi no Takan *: Names appear on wooden tablets excavated from the Heijō Palace site. * Kuramochi no Wakamaru *: Appears on labels excavated from the Heijō Palace site.
== References == {{reflist}}
== Sources == * ''Nihon Shoki,'' Volumes 3 and 5, {{ill|Iwanami Bunko|ja|岩波文庫}}, 1994–1995. * {{ill|Ujitani Mitsuru|ja|宇治谷孟}}, ''Nihon Shoki: Complete Modern Translation,'' Vols. 1–2, {{ill|Kōdansha Gakujutsu Bunko|ja|講談社学術文庫}}, 1988. * {{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}} Volumes 1 and 5, {{ill|Iwanami Shoten|ja|岩波書店}}, 1989, 1998. * {{ill|Saeki Ariyoshi|ja|佐伯有清}}, ''Japanese Ancient Clan Encyclopedia,'' 2015. * {{ill|Sakamoto Tarō|ja|坂本太郎}}, ''Japanese Ancient Clan Names Dictionary,'' {{ill|Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|ja|吉川弘文館}}, 1990. * {{ill|Takeuchi Rizo|ja|竹内理三}}, ''Japanese Ancient Names Dictionary,'' Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1972.

revision.text:
'''Kuramochi Clan''' (車持氏) is a clan that takes its name, "Kuramochi," from its family lineage. This clan oversaw the occupational group ({{ill|Shinabe|ja|品部}}) known as the {{ill|Kuramochibe|ja|車持部}}, which managed specific duties.
== Overview ==
According to the {{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|ja|新撰姓氏録}}, under the Left Capital section of imperial lineage, the Kuramochi clan shared the same ancestry as the {{ill|Kamitsukeno clan|ja|上毛野氏}}. They were descendants of {{ill|Toyokiirihiko-no-mikoto|ja|豊城入彦命}}, specifically from his eighth-generation descendant Isasa no Kimi. During the reign of {{ill|Emperor Yūryaku|ja|雄略天皇}}, the clan earned the surname "Kuramochi no Kimi" after offering vehicles to the court.
The {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}} mentions an event during the 5th year of {{ill|Emperor Richū|ja|履中天皇}} (404 CE, estimated). When Emperor Richū mourned the death of his consort, {{ill|Kurohime|ja|葛城黒媛}}, divine punishment was blamed on Kuramochi no Kimi. He had misused resources assigned to {{ill|Kuramochibe|ja|車持部}} for investigations in {{ill|Tsukushi Province|ja|筑紫国}} and confiscated goods dedicated to {{ill|Munakata Shrine|ja|宗像神社}}. As a result, the emperor condemned Kuramochi no Kimi, ordering purification rites at Nagasunozaki (now Nagasu, {{ill|Amagasaki|ja|尼崎市}}), and banned him from managing Kuramochibe in Tsukushi.<ref>''Nihon Shoki,'' Emperor Richū, 5th year, 10th month, 11th day entry.</ref>
The ''Nihon Shoki,'' citing the "Baekje Annals," records that in the 3rd year of {{ill|Emperor Keitai|ja|継体天皇}} (509), Kuramochi no Kimi (久羅麻致支弥, Kuramachikimi) was sent to {{ill|Baekje|ja|百済}} to return refugees who had fled to {{ill|Mimana|ja|任那}} back to their homeland and re-register them.<ref>{{Cite web|title=久羅麻致支弥|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/久羅麻致支弥-1072490|publisher=Kotobank}}</ref>
During the {{ill|Asuka period|ja|飛鳥時代}}, Kuramochi Kunikko’s daughter, {{ill|Kuramochi Yoshikome|ja|車持与志古娘}}, married {{ill|Nakatomi no Kamatari|ja|中臣鎌足}}, giving birth to {{ill|Fujiwara no Fuhito|ja|藤原不比等}}.
In the {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}}, {{ill|Kuramochi Chitose|ja|車持千年}} served as a court poet for {{ill|Empress Genmei|ja|元明天皇}} and {{ill|Emperor Shōmu|ja|聖武天皇}}.
According to the {{ill|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|ja|日本三代実録}}, compiled in 882, the Kuramochi clan served under the {{ill|Ritsuryō|ja|律令}} system as subordinates in the {{ill|Ministry of the Imperial Household (Ritsuryō)|ja|宮内省}}’s {{ill|Shudenryō|ja|主殿寮}}. Their duties included managing imperial palanquins. The ''Engishiki'' mentions a member of the Kuramochi clan performing ceremonial roles during the {{ill|Daijōsai|ja|大嘗祭}}.<ref>''Engishiki,'' vol. 7, ''Shinji,'' "Daijōsai," Article 40.</ref>
The ''Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku'' also states that in 877, the Kuramochi clan and five other clans—Kamitsukeno, Ōno, Ikeda, Sami, and Shimotsukeno—were all descendants of Emperor Sujin. According to the ''Shoku Nihongi,'' a petition submitted by {{ill|Sumiyoshi no Tsunamori|ja|住吉綱主}} in 791 noted that these clans were settled in the eastern provinces, taking surnames based on their places of residence. Archaeological evidence from wooden tablets excavated from the Fujiwara Palace indicates that Kamitsukeno’s territory included a district named "Kuramochi District" until the late 7th century.
The {{ill|Hodota Kofun Group|ja|保渡田古墳群}} in {{ill|Takasaki|ja|高崎市}}, Gunma Prefecture, includes three keyhole-shaped burial mounds constructed between the late 5th and early 6th centuries. These are believed to be tombs of regional leaders linked to the formation of the Keno Province. The nearby {{ill|Mitsudera Site|ja|三ツ寺Ⅰ遺跡}} further highlights connections between these clans and the Yamato court.
The Kuramochi clan’s {{ill|Kabane|ja|姓}} was originally "Kimi," later elevated to "Ason" under the {{ill|Eight Prince Ranks|ja|八色の姓}} reform initiated by {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|天武天皇}}.<ref>''Nihon Shoki,'' Emperor Tenmu, 13th year, 11th month, 1st day entry.</ref>
== Kuramochi Clan in Wooden Tablets and Registers == * Kuramochi Ason (Name unknown), Kuramochi Ason Michimichi *: Names appear on wooden tablets excavated from the Dazaifu government complex. * Kuramochi no Kimi Nemaro, Kuramochi no Kimi Ikusa, Kuramochi no Kimi Hitsuji *: Appears in the fragmentary register "Bungo Province Nakatsu District Household Registry of 702." * Kuramochi no Sazumaro, Kuramochi no Takan *: Names appear on wooden tablets excavated from the Heijō Palace site. * Kuramochi no Wakamaru *: Appears on labels excavated from the Heijō Palace site.
== References == {{reflist}}
== Sources == * ''Nihon Shoki,'' Volumes 3 and 5, {{ill|Iwanami Bunko|ja|岩波文庫}}, 1994–1995. * {{ill|Ujitani Mitsuru|ja|宇治谷孟}}, ''Nihon Shoki: Complete Modern Translation,'' Vols. 1–2, {{ill|Kōdansha Gakujutsu Bunko|ja|講談社学術文庫}}, 1988. * {{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}} Volumes 1 and 5, {{ill|Iwanami Shoten|ja|岩波書店}}, 1989, 1998. * {{ill|Saeki Ariyoshi|ja|佐伯有清}}, ''Japanese Ancient Clan Encyclopedia,'' 2015. * {{ill|Sakamoto Tarō|ja|坂本太郎}}, ''Japanese Ancient Clan Names Dictionary,'' {{ill|Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|ja|吉川弘文館}}, 1990. * {{ill|Takeuchi Rizo|ja|竹内理三}}, ''Japanese Ancient Names Dictionary,'' Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1972.

== Kuramochi Clan in Wooden Tablets and Registers == * Kuramochi Ason (Name unknown), Kuramochi Ason Michimichi *: Names appear on wooden tablets excavated from the Dazaifu government complex. * Kuramochi no Kimi Nemaro, Kuramochi no Kimi Ikusa, Kuramochi no Kimi Hitsuji *: Appears in the fragmentary register "Bungo Province Nakatsu District Household Registry of 702." * Kuramochi no Sazumaro, Kuramochi no Takan *: Names appear on wooden tablets excavated from the Heijō Palace site. * Kuramochi no Wakamaru *: Appears on labels excavated from the Heijō Palace site.

revision.text:
'''Kuramochi Clan''' (車持氏) is a clan that takes its name, "Kuramochi," from its family lineage. This clan oversaw the occupational group ({{ill|Shinabe|ja|品部}}) known as the {{ill|Kuramochibe|ja|車持部}}, which managed specific duties.
== Overview ==
According to the {{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|ja|新撰姓氏録}}, under the Left Capital section of imperial lineage, the Kuramochi clan shared the same ancestry as the {{ill|Kamitsukeno clan|ja|上毛野氏}}. They were descendants of {{ill|Toyokiirihiko-no-mikoto|ja|豊城入彦命}}, specifically from his eighth-generation descendant Isasa no Kimi. During the reign of {{ill|Emperor Yūryaku|ja|雄略天皇}}, the clan earned the surname "Kuramochi no Kimi" after offering vehicles to the court.
The {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}} mentions an event during the 5th year of {{ill|Emperor Richū|ja|履中天皇}} (404 CE, estimated). When Emperor Richū mourned the death of his consort, {{ill|Kurohime|ja|葛城黒媛}}, divine punishment was blamed on Kuramochi no Kimi. He had misused resources assigned to {{ill|Kuramochibe|ja|車持部}} for investigations in {{ill|Tsukushi Province|ja|筑紫国}} and confiscated goods dedicated to {{ill|Munakata Shrine|ja|宗像神社}}. As a result, the emperor condemned Kuramochi no Kimi, ordering purification rites at Nagasunozaki (now Nagasu, {{ill|Amagasaki|ja|尼崎市}}), and banned him from managing Kuramochibe in Tsukushi.<ref>''Nihon Shoki,'' Emperor Richū, 5th year, 10th month, 11th day entry.</ref>
The ''Nihon Shoki,'' citing the "Baekje Annals," records that in the 3rd year of {{ill|Emperor Keitai|ja|継体天皇}} (509), Kuramochi no Kimi (久羅麻致支弥, Kuramachikimi) was sent to {{ill|Baekje|ja|百済}} to return refugees who had fled to {{ill|Mimana|ja|任那}} back to their homeland and re-register them.<ref>{{Cite web|title=久羅麻致支弥|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/久羅麻致支弥-1072490|publisher=Kotobank}}</ref>
During the {{ill|Asuka period|ja|飛鳥時代}}, Kuramochi Kunikko’s daughter, {{ill|Kuramochi Yoshikome|ja|車持与志古娘}}, married {{ill|Nakatomi no Kamatari|ja|中臣鎌足}}, giving birth to {{ill|Fujiwara no Fuhito|ja|藤原不比等}}.
In the {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}}, {{ill|Kuramochi Chitose|ja|車持千年}} served as a court poet for {{ill|Empress Genmei|ja|元明天皇}} and {{ill|Emperor Shōmu|ja|聖武天皇}}.
According to the {{ill|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|ja|日本三代実録}}, compiled in 882, the Kuramochi clan served under the {{ill|Ritsuryō|ja|律令}} system as subordinates in the {{ill|Ministry of the Imperial Household (Ritsuryō)|ja|宮内省}}’s {{ill|Shudenryō|ja|主殿寮}}. Their duties included managing imperial palanquins. The ''Engishiki'' mentions a member of the Kuramochi clan performing ceremonial roles during the {{ill|Daijōsai|ja|大嘗祭}}.<ref>''Engishiki,'' vol. 7, ''Shinji,'' "Daijōsai," Article 40.</ref>
The ''Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku'' also states that in 877, the Kuramochi clan and five other clans—Kamitsukeno, Ōno, Ikeda, Sami, and Shimotsukeno—were all descendants of Emperor Sujin. According to the ''Shoku Nihongi,'' a petition submitted by {{ill|Sumiyoshi no Tsunamori|ja|住吉綱主}} in 791 noted that these clans were settled in the eastern provinces, taking surnames based on their places of residence. Archaeological evidence from wooden tablets excavated from the Fujiwara Palace indicates that Kamitsukeno’s territory included a district named "Kuramochi District" until the late 7th century.
The {{ill|Hodota Kofun Group|ja|保渡田古墳群}} in {{ill|Takasaki|ja|高崎市}}, Gunma Prefecture, includes three keyhole-shaped burial mounds constructed between the late 5th and early 6th centuries. These are believed to be tombs of regional leaders linked to the formation of the Keno Province. The nearby {{ill|Mitsudera Site|ja|三ツ寺Ⅰ遺跡}} further highlights connections between these clans and the Yamato court.
The Kuramochi clan’s {{ill|Kabane|ja|姓}} was originally "Kimi," later elevated to "Ason" under the {{ill|Eight Prince Ranks|ja|八色の姓}} reform initiated by {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|天武天皇}}.<ref>''Nihon Shoki,'' Emperor Tenmu, 13th year, 11th month, 1st day entry.</ref>
== Kuramochi Clan in Wooden Tablets and Registers == * Kuramochi Ason (Name unknown), Kuramochi Ason Michimichi *: Names appear on wooden tablets excavated from the Dazaifu government complex. * Kuramochi no Kimi Nemaro, Kuramochi no Kimi Ikusa, Kuramochi no Kimi Hitsuji *: Appears in the fragmentary register "Bungo Province Nakatsu District Household Registry of 702." * Kuramochi no Sazumaro, Kuramochi no Takan *: Names appear on wooden tablets excavated from the Heijō Palace site. * Kuramochi no Wakamaru *: Appears on labels excavated from the Heijō Palace site.
== References == {{reflist}}
== Sources == * ''Nihon Shoki,'' Volumes 3 and 5, {{ill|Iwanami Bunko|ja|岩波文庫}}, 1994–1995. * {{ill|Ujitani Mitsuru|ja|宇治谷孟}}, ''Nihon Shoki: Complete Modern Translation,'' Vols. 1–2, {{ill|Kōdansha Gakujutsu Bunko|ja|講談社学術文庫}}, 1988. * {{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}} Volumes 1 and 5, {{ill|Iwanami Shoten|ja|岩波書店}}, 1989, 1998. * {{ill|Saeki Ariyoshi|ja|佐伯有清}}, ''Japanese Ancient Clan Encyclopedia,'' 2015. * {{ill|Sakamoto Tarō|ja|坂本太郎}}, ''Japanese Ancient Clan Names Dictionary,'' {{ill|Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|ja|吉川弘文館}}, 1990. * {{ill|Takeuchi Rizo|ja|竹内理三}}, ''Japanese Ancient Names Dictionary,'' Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1972.
{{Japanese-history-stub}} {{people-stub}}

== Sources == * ''Nihon Shoki,'' Volumes 3 and 5, {{ill|Iwanami Bunko|ja|岩波文庫}}, 1994–1995. * {{ill|Ujitani Mitsuru|ja|宇治谷孟}}, ''Nihon Shoki: Complete Modern Translation,'' Vols. 1–2, {{ill|Kōdansha Gakujutsu Bunko|ja|講談社学術文庫}}, 1988. * {{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}} Volumes 1 and 5, {{ill|Iwanami Shoten|ja|岩波書店}}, 1989, 1998. * {{ill|Saeki Ariyoshi|ja|佐伯有清}}, ''Japanese Ancient Clan Encyclopedia,'' 2015. * {{ill|Sakamoto Tarō|ja|坂本太郎}}, ''Japanese Ancient Clan Names Dictionary,'' {{ill|Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|ja|吉川弘文館}}, 1990. * {{ill|Takeuchi Rizo|ja|竹内理三}}, ''Japanese Ancient Names Dictionary,'' Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1972.

revision.text:
'''Kuramochi Clan''' (車持氏) is a clan that takes its name, "Kuramochi," from its family lineage. This clan oversaw the occupational group ({{ill|Shinabe|ja|品部}}) known as the {{ill|Kuramochibe|ja|車持部}}, which managed specific duties.
== Overview ==
According to the {{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|ja|新撰姓氏録}}, under the Left Capital section of imperial lineage, the Kuramochi clan shared the same ancestry as the {{ill|Kamitsukeno clan|ja|上毛野氏}}. They were descendants of {{ill|Toyokiirihiko-no-mikoto|ja|豊城入彦命}}, specifically from his eighth-generation descendant Isasa no Kimi. During the reign of {{ill|Emperor Yūryaku|ja|雄略天皇}}, the clan earned the surname "Kuramochi no Kimi" after offering vehicles to the court.
The {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}} mentions an event during the 5th year of {{ill|Emperor Richū|ja|履中天皇}} (404 CE, estimated). When Emperor Richū mourned the death of his consort, {{ill|Kurohime|ja|葛城黒媛}}, divine punishment was blamed on Kuramochi no Kimi. He had misused resources assigned to {{ill|Kuramochibe|ja|車持部}} for investigations in {{ill|Tsukushi Province|ja|筑紫国}} and confiscated goods dedicated to {{ill|Munakata Shrine|ja|宗像神社}}. As a result, the emperor condemned Kuramochi no Kimi, ordering purification rites at Nagasunozaki (now Nagasu, {{ill|Amagasaki|ja|尼崎市}}), and banned him from managing Kuramochibe in Tsukushi.<ref>''Nihon Shoki,'' Emperor Richū, 5th year, 10th month, 11th day entry.</ref>
The ''Nihon Shoki,'' citing the "Baekje Annals," records that in the 3rd year of {{ill|Emperor Keitai|ja|継体天皇}} (509), Kuramochi no Kimi (久羅麻致支弥, Kuramachikimi) was sent to {{ill|Baekje|ja|百済}} to return refugees who had fled to {{ill|Mimana|ja|任那}} back to their homeland and re-register them.<ref>{{Cite web|title=久羅麻致支弥|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/久羅麻致支弥-1072490|publisher=Kotobank}}</ref>
During the {{ill|Asuka period|ja|飛鳥時代}}, Kuramochi Kunikko’s daughter, {{ill|Kuramochi Yoshikome|ja|車持与志古娘}}, married {{ill|Nakatomi no Kamatari|ja|中臣鎌足}}, giving birth to {{ill|Fujiwara no Fuhito|ja|藤原不比等}}.
In the {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}}, {{ill|Kuramochi Chitose|ja|車持千年}} served as a court poet for {{ill|Empress Genmei|ja|元明天皇}} and {{ill|Emperor Shōmu|ja|聖武天皇}}.
According to the {{ill|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|ja|日本三代実録}}, compiled in 882, the Kuramochi clan served under the {{ill|Ritsuryō|ja|律令}} system as subordinates in the {{ill|Ministry of the Imperial Household (Ritsuryō)|ja|宮内省}}’s {{ill|Shudenryō|ja|主殿寮}}. Their duties included managing imperial palanquins. The ''Engishiki'' mentions a member of the Kuramochi clan performing ceremonial roles during the {{ill|Daijōsai|ja|大嘗祭}}.<ref>''Engishiki,'' vol. 7, ''Shinji,'' "Daijōsai," Article 40.</ref>
The ''Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku'' also states that in 877, the Kuramochi clan and five other clans—Kamitsukeno, Ōno, Ikeda, Sami, and Shimotsukeno—were all descendants of Emperor Sujin. According to the ''Shoku Nihongi,'' a petition submitted by {{ill|Sumiyoshi no Tsunamori|ja|住吉綱主}} in 791 noted that these clans were settled in the eastern provinces, taking surnames based on their places of residence. Archaeological evidence from wooden tablets excavated from the Fujiwara Palace indicates that Kamitsukeno’s territory included a district named "Kuramochi District" until the late 7th century.
The {{ill|Hodota Kofun Group|ja|保渡田古墳群}} in {{ill|Takasaki|ja|高崎市}}, Gunma Prefecture, includes three keyhole-shaped burial mounds constructed between the late 5th and early 6th centuries. These are believed to be tombs of regional leaders linked to the formation of the Keno Province. The nearby {{ill|Mitsudera Site|ja|三ツ寺Ⅰ遺跡}} further highlights connections between these clans and the Yamato court.
The Kuramochi clan’s {{ill|Kabane|ja|姓}} was originally "Kimi," later elevated to "Ason" under the {{ill|Eight Prince Ranks|ja|八色の姓}} reform initiated by {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|天武天皇}}.<ref>''Nihon Shoki,'' Emperor Tenmu, 13th year, 11th month, 1st day entry.</ref>
== Kuramochi Clan in Wooden Tablets and Registers == * Kuramochi Ason (Name unknown), Kuramochi Ason Michimichi *: Names appear on wooden tablets excavated from the Dazaifu government complex. * Kuramochi no Kimi Nemaro, Kuramochi no Kimi Ikusa, Kuramochi no Kimi Hitsuji *: Appears in the fragmentary register "Bungo Province Nakatsu District Household Registry of 702." * Kuramochi no Sazumaro, Kuramochi no Takan *: Names appear on wooden tablets excavated from the Heijō Palace site. * Kuramochi no Wakamaru *: Appears on labels excavated from the Heijō Palace site.
== References == {{reflist}}
== Sources == * ''Nihon Shoki,'' Volumes 3 and 5, {{ill|Iwanami Bunko|ja|岩波文庫}}, 1994–1995. * {{ill|Ujitani Mitsuru|ja|宇治谷孟}}, ''Nihon Shoki: Complete Modern Translation,'' Vols. 1–2, {{ill|Kōdansha Gakujutsu Bunko|ja|講談社学術文庫}}, 1988. * {{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}} Volumes 1 and 5, {{ill|Iwanami Shoten|ja|岩波書店}}, 1989, 1998. * {{ill|Saeki Ariyoshi|ja|佐伯有清}}, ''Japanese Ancient Clan Encyclopedia,'' 2015. * {{ill|Sakamoto Tarō|ja|坂本太郎}}, ''Japanese Ancient Clan Names Dictionary,'' {{ill|Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|ja|吉川弘文館}}, 1990. * {{ill|Takeuchi Rizo|ja|竹内理三}}, ''Japanese Ancient Names Dictionary,'' Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1972.
{{Japanese-history-stub}} {{people-stub}}

During the {{ill|Asuka period|ja|飛鳥時代}}, Kuramochi Kunikko’s daughter, {{ill|Kuramochi Yoshikome|ja|車持与志古娘}}, married {{ill|Nakatomi no Kamatari|ja|中臣鎌足}}, giving birth to {{ill|Fujiwara no Fuhito|ja|藤原不比等}}.
In the {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}}, {{ill|Kuramochi Chitose|ja|車持千年}} served as a court poet for {{ill|Empress Genmei|ja|元明天皇}} and {{ill|Emperor Shōmu|ja|聖武天皇}}.
According to the {{ill|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|ja|日本三代実録}}, compiled in 882, the Kuramochi clan served under the {{ill|Ritsuryō|ja|律令}} system as subordinates in the {{ill|Ministry of the Imperial Household (Ritsuryō)|ja|宮内省}}’s {{ill|Shudenryō|ja|主殿寮}}. Their duties included managing imperial palanquins. The ''Engishiki'' mentions a member of the Kuramochi clan performing ceremonial roles during the {{ill|Daijōsai|ja|大嘗祭}}.<ref>''Engishiki,'' vol. 7, ''Shinji,'' "Daijōsai," Article 40.</ref>
The ''Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku'' also states that in 877, the Kuramochi clan and five other clans—Kamitsukeno, Ōno, Ikeda, Sami, and Shimotsukeno—were all descendants of Emperor Sujin. According to the ''Shoku Nihongi,'' a petition submitted by {{ill|Sumiyoshi no Tsunamori|ja|住吉綱主}} in 791 noted that these clans were settled in the eastern provinces, taking surnames based on their places of residence. Archaeological evidence from wooden tablets excavated from the Fujiwara Palace indicates that Kamitsukeno’s territory included a district named "Kuramochi District" until the late 7th century.
The {{ill|Hodota Kofun Group|ja|保渡田古墳群}} in {{ill|Takasaki|ja|高崎市}}, Gunma Prefecture, includes three keyhole-shaped burial mounds constructed between the late 5th and early 6th centuries. These are believed to be tombs of regional leaders linked to the formation of the Keno Province. The nearby {{ill|Mitsudera Site|ja|三ツ寺Ⅰ遺跡}} further highlights connections between these clans and the Yamato court.
The Kuramochi clan’s {{ill|Kabane|ja|姓}} was originally "Kimi," later elevated to "Ason" under the {{ill|Eight Prince Ranks|ja|八色の姓}} reform initiated by {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|天武天皇}}.<ref>''Nihon Shoki,'' Emperor Tenmu, 13th year, 11th month, 1st day entry.</ref>
== Kuramochi Clan in Wooden Tablets and Registers == * Kuramochi Ason (Name unknown), Kuramochi Ason Michimichi *: Names appear on wooden tablets excavated from the Dazaifu government complex. * Kuramochi no Kimi Nemaro, Kuramochi no Kimi Ikusa, Kuramochi no Kimi Hitsuji *: Appears in the fragmentary register "Bungo Province Nakatsu District Household Registry of 702." * Kuramochi no Sazumaro, Kuramochi no Takan *: Names appear on wooden tablets excavated from the Heijō Palace site. * Kuramochi no Wakamaru *: Appears on labels excavated from the Heijō Palace site.
== References == {{reflist}}
== Sources == * ''Nihon Shoki,'' Volumes 3 and 5, {{ill|Iwanami Bunko|ja|岩波文庫}}, 1994–1995. * {{ill|Ujitani Mitsuru|ja|宇治谷孟}}, ''Nihon Shoki: Complete Modern Translation,'' Vols. 1–2, {{ill|Kōdansha Gakujutsu Bunko|ja|講談社学術文庫}}, 1988. * {{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}} Volumes 1 and 5, {{ill|Iwanami Shoten|ja|岩波書店}}, 1989, 1998. * {{ill|Saeki Ariyoshi|ja|佐伯有清}}, ''Japanese Ancient Clan Encyclopedia,'' 2015. * {{ill|Sakamoto Tarō|ja|坂本太郎}}, ''Japanese Ancient Clan Names Dictionary,'' {{ill|Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|ja|吉川弘文館}}, 1990. * {{ill|Takeuchi Rizo|ja|竹内理三}}, ''Japanese Ancient Names Dictionary,'' Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1972.

'''Kuramochi Clan''' (車持氏) is a clan that takes its name, "Kuramochi," from its family lineage. This clan oversaw the occupational group ({{ill|Shinabe|ja|品部}}) known as the {{ill|Kuramochibe|ja|車持部}}, which managed specific duties.
== Overview ==
According to the {{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|ja|新撰姓氏録}}, under the Left Capital section of imperial lineage, the Kuramochi clan shared the same ancestry as the {{ill|Kamitsukeno clan|ja|上毛野氏}}. They were descendants of {{ill|Toyokiirihiko-no-mikoto|ja|豊城入彦命}}, specifically from his eighth-generation descendant Isasa no Kimi. During the reign of {{ill|Emperor Yūryaku|ja|雄略天皇}}, the clan earned the surname "Kuramochi no Kimi" after offering vehicles to the court.
The {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}} mentions an event during the 5th year of {{ill|Emperor Richū|ja|履中天皇}} (404 CE, estimated). When Emperor Richū mourned the death of his consort, {{ill|Kurohime|ja|葛城黒媛}}, divine punishment was blamed on Kuramochi no Kimi. He had misused resources assigned to {{ill|Kuramochibe|ja|車持部}} for investigations in {{ill|Tsukushi Province|ja|筑紫国}} and confiscated goods dedicated to {{ill|Munakata Shrine|ja|宗像神社}}. As a result, the emperor condemned Kuramochi no Kimi, ordering purification rites at Nagasunozaki (now Nagasu, {{ill|Amagasaki|ja|尼崎市}}), and banned him from managing Kuramochibe in Tsukushi.<ref>''Nihon Shoki,'' Emperor Richū, 5th year, 10th month, 11th day entry.</ref>
The ''Nihon Shoki,'' citing the "Baekje Annals," records that in the 3rd year of {{ill|Emperor Keitai|ja|継体天皇}} (509), Kuramochi no Kimi (久羅麻致支弥, Kuramachikimi) was sent to {{ill|Baekje|ja|百済}} to return refugees who had fled to {{ill|Mimana|ja|任那}} back to their homeland and re-register them.<ref>{{Cite web|title=久羅麻致支弥|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/久羅麻致支弥-1072490|publisher=Kotobank}}</ref>
During the {{ill|Asuka period|ja|飛鳥時代}}, Kuramochi Kunikko’s daughter, {{ill|Kuramochi Yoshikome|ja|車持与志古娘}}, married {{ill|Nakatomi no Kamatari|ja|中臣鎌足}}, giving birth to {{ill|Fujiwara no Fuhito|ja|藤原不比等}}.
In the {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}}, {{ill|Kuramochi Chitose|ja|車持千年}} served as a court poet for {{ill|Empress Genmei|ja|元明天皇}} and {{ill|Emperor Shōmu|ja|聖武天皇}}.
According to the {{ill|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|ja|日本三代実録}}, compiled in 882, the Kuramochi clan served under the {{ill|Ritsuryō|ja|律令}} system as subordinates in the {{ill|Ministry of the Imperial Household (Ritsuryō)|ja|宮内省}}’s {{ill|Shudenryō|ja|主殿寮}}. Their duties included managing imperial palanquins. The ''Engishiki'' mentions a member of the Kuramochi clan performing ceremonial roles during the {{ill|Daijōsai|ja|大嘗祭}}.<ref>''Engishiki,'' vol. 7, ''Shinji,'' "Daijōsai," Article 40.</ref>
The ''Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku'' also states that in 877, the Kuramochi clan and five other clans—Kamitsukeno, Ōno, Ikeda, Sami, and Shimotsukeno—were all descendants of Emperor Sujin. According to the ''Shoku Nihongi,'' a petition submitted by {{ill|Sumiyoshi no Tsunamori|ja|住吉綱主}} in 791 noted that these clans were settled in the eastern provinces, taking surnames based on their places of residence. Archaeological evidence from wooden tablets excavated from the Fujiwara Palace indicates that Kamitsukeno’s territory included a district named "Kuramochi District" until the late 7th century.
The {{ill|Hodota Kofun Group|ja|保渡田古墳群}} in {{ill|Takasaki|ja|高崎市}}, Gunma Prefecture, includes three keyhole-shaped burial mounds constructed between the late 5th and early 6th centuries. These are believed to be tombs of regional leaders linked to the formation of the Keno Province. The nearby {{ill|Mitsudera Site|ja|三ツ寺Ⅰ遺跡}} further highlights connections between these clans and the Yamato court.
The Kuramochi clan’s {{ill|Kabane|ja|姓}} was originally "Kimi," later elevated to "Ason" under the {{ill|Eight Prince Ranks|ja|八色の姓}} reform initiated by {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|天武天皇}}.<ref>''Nihon Shoki,'' Emperor Tenmu, 13th year, 11th month, 1st day entry.</ref>
