<!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> {{wikidata link|Q120336431}} [[Category:Clans by region]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:Japanese clans]] [[Category:Kurumamochi Clan]] [[Category:Uji]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata no short description]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:車持氏]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:車持氏]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:車持氏]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q120336431]] Wikidata name: Kurumamochi clan [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:45, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Kuramochi Clan''' (車持氏) is a clan that takes its name, "Kuramochi," from its family lineage. This clan oversaw the occupational group ([[:en:Shinabe clans|Shinabe]]) known as the {{ill|Kuramochibe|ja|車持部|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuramochibe|qq=}}, which managed specific duties.
== Overview ==
According to the [[:en:Shinsen Shōjiroku|Shinsen Shōjiroku]], under the Left Capital section of imperial lineage, the Kuramochi clan shared the same ancestry as the {{ill|Kamitsukeno clan|ja|上毛野氏|1=Kamitsukeno clan|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamitsukeno clan|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamitsukeno clan|qq=}}. They were descendants of {{ill|Toyokiirihiko-no-mikoto|ja|豊城入彦命|1=Toyokiirihiko-no-Mikoto|1=Toyokiirihiko-no-Mikoto|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyokiirihiko-no-mikoto|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyokiirihiko-no-Mikoto|qq=}}, specifically from his eighth-generation descendant Isasa no Kimi. During the reign of [[:en:Emperor Yūryaku|Emperor Yūryaku]], the clan earned the surname "Kuramochi no Kimi" after offering vehicles to the court.
The [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]] mentions an event during the 5th year of [[:en:Emperor Richū|Emperor Richū]] (404 CE, estimated). When Emperor Richū mourned the death of his consort, {{ill|Kurohime|ja|葛城黒媛|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kurohime|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, divine punishment was blamed on Kuramochi no Kimi. He had misused resources assigned to {{ill|Kuramochibe|ja|車持部|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuramochibe|qq=}} for investigations in [[:en:Tsukushi Province|Tsukushi Province]] and confiscated goods dedicated to {{ill|Munakata Shrine|ja|宗像神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Munakata Shrine|1=Munakata shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11451876}}. As a result, the emperor condemned Kuramochi no Kimi, ordering purification rites at Nagasunozaki (now Nagasu, [[:en:Amagasaki|Amagasaki]]), and banned him from managing Kuramochibe in Tsukushi.<ref>''Nihon Shoki,'' Emperor Richū, 5th year, 10th month, 11th day entry.</ref>
The ''Nihon Shoki,'' citing the "Baekje Annals," records that in the 3rd year of [[:en:Emperor Keitai|Emperor Keitai]] (509), Kuramochi no Kimi (久羅麻致支弥, Kuramachikimi) was sent to [[:en:Baekje|Baekje]] to return refugees who had fled to [[:en:Mimana|Mimana]] back to their homeland and re-register them.<ref>{{Cite web|title=久羅麻致支弥|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/久羅麻致支弥-1072490|publisher=Kotobank}}</ref>
During the [[:en:Asuka period|Asuka period]], Kuramochi Kunikko’s daughter, {{ill|Kuramochi Yoshikome|ja|車持与志古娘|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuramochi Yoshikome|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, married [[:en:Nakatomi no Kamatari|Nakatomi no Kamatari]], giving birth to [[:en:Fujiwara no Fuhito|Fujiwara no Fuhito]].
In the [[:en:Nara period|Nara period]], {{ill|Kuramochi Chitose|ja|車持千年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuramochi Chitose|qq=}} served as a court poet for [[:en:Empress Genmei|Empress Genmei]] and [[:en:Emperor Shōmu|Emperor Shōmu]].
According to the [[:en:Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]], compiled in 882, the Kuramochi clan served under the [[:en:Traditional Chinese law|Ritsuryō]] system as subordinates in the [[:en:Ministry of the Imperial Household|Ministry of the Imperial Household (Ritsuryō)]]’s {{ill|Shudenryō|ja|主殿寮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shudenryō|qq=}}. Their duties included managing imperial palanquins. The ''Engishiki'' mentions a member of the Kuramochi clan performing ceremonial roles during the [[:en:Daijosai|Daijosai]].<ref>''Engishiki,'' vol. 7, ''Shinji,'' "Daijōsai," Article 40.</ref>
The ''Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku'' also states that in 877, the Kuramochi clan and five other clans—Kamitsukeno, Ōno, Ikeda, Sami, and Shimotsukeno—were all descendants of Emperor Sujin. According to the ''Shoku Nihongi,'' a petition submitted by {{ill|Sumiyoshi no Tsunamori|ja|住吉綱主|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sumiyoshi no Tsunamori|qq=}} in 791 noted that these clans were settled in the eastern provinces, taking surnames based on their places of residence. Archaeological evidence from wooden tablets excavated from the Fujiwara Palace indicates that Kamitsukeno’s territory included a district named "Kuramochi District" until the late 7th century.
The [[:en:Hodota Kofun Cluster|Hodota Kofun Group]] in [[:en:Takasaki|Takasaki]], Gunma Prefecture, includes three keyhole-shaped Kofuns constructed between the late 5th and early 6th centuries. These are believed to be tombs of regional leaders linked to the formation of the Keno Province. The nearby {{ill|Mitsudera Site|ja|三ツ寺Ⅰ遺跡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mitsudera Site|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} further highlights connections between these clans and the Yamato court.
The Kuramochi clan’s {{ill|Kabane|ja|姓|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kabane|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} was originally "Kimi," later elevated to "Ason" under the {{ill|Eight Prince Ranks|ja|八色の姓|1=Eight Kabane system|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eight Prince Ranks|13=User:Immanuelle/Eight Kabane system|qq=}} reform initiated by {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|1=Tenmu|2=ja|3=天武天皇|ja|Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu|2=ja|3=天武天皇|1=Emperor Tenmu|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Tenmu|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Tenmu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q335181}}.<ref>''Nihon Shoki,'' Emperor Tenmu, 13th year, 11th month, 1st day entry.</ref>
== Kuramochi Clan in Wooden Tablets and Registers == * Kuramochi Ason (Name unknown), Kuramochi Ason Michimichi *: Names appear on wooden tablets excavated from the Dazaifu government complex. * Kuramochi no Kimi Nemaro, Kuramochi no Kimi Ikusa, Kuramochi no Kimi Hitsuji *: Appears in the fragmentary register "Bungo Province Nakatsu District Household Registry of 702." * Kuramochi no Sazumaro, Kuramochi no Takan *: Names appear on wooden tablets excavated from the Heijō Palace site. * Kuramochi no Wakamaru *: Appears on labels excavated from the Heijō Palace site.
== References == {{reflist}}
== Sources == * ''Nihon Shoki,'' Volumes 3 and 5, {{ill|Iwanami Bunko|ja|岩波文庫|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwanami Bunko|1=Iwanami Bunko|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11475322}}, 1994–1995. * {{ill|Ujitani Mitsuru|ja|宇治谷孟|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ujitani Mitsuru|qq=}}, ''Nihon Shoki: Complete Modern Translation,'' Vols. 1–2, {{ill|Kōdansha Gakujutsu Bunko|ja|講談社学術文庫|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōdansha Gakujutsu Bunko|1=Kōdansha gakujutsu bunko|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11632220}}, 1988. * [[:en:Shoku Nihongi|Shoku Nihongi]] Volumes 1 and 5, [[:en:Iwanami Shoten|Iwanami Shoten]], 1989, 1998. * {{ill|Saeki Ariyoshi|ja|佐伯有清|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saeki Ariyoshi|qq=}}, ''Japanese Ancient Clan Encyclopedia,'' 2015. * {{ill|Sakamoto Tarō|ja|坂本太郎|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakamoto Tarō|1=Sakamoto Tarō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11425251}}, ''Japanese Ancient Clan Names Dictionary,'' {{ill|Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|ja|吉川弘文館|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|1=Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4346755}}, 1990. * [[:en:Rizō Takeuchi|Takeuchi Rizo]], ''Japanese Ancient Names Dictionary,'' Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1972.

This clan oversaw the occupational group ({{ill|Shinabe clans|en|Shinabe clans|WD=Q11418456|lt=Shinabe}}) known as the {{ill|Kuramochibe|ja|車持部|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuramochibe|qq=}}, which managed specific duties.
== Overview ==
According to the {{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|en|Shinsen Shōjiroku|WD=Q1045362|lt=Shinsen Shōjiroku}}, under the Left Capital section of imperial lineage, the Kuramochi clan shared the same ancestry as the {{ill|Kamitsukeno clan|ja|上毛野氏|1=Kamitsukeno clan|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamitsukeno clan|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamitsukeno clan|qq=}}. They were descendants of {{ill|Toyokiirihiko-no-mikoto|ja|豊城入彦命|1=Toyokiirihiko-no-Mikoto|1=Toyokiirihiko-no-Mikoto|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyokiirihiko-no-mikoto|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyokiirihiko-no-Mikoto|qq=}}, specifically from his eighth-generation descendant Isasa no Kimi. During the reign of {{ill|Emperor Yūryaku|en|Emperor Yūryaku|WD=Q357235|lt=Emperor Yūryaku}}, the clan earned the surname "Kuramochi no Kimi" after offering vehicles to the court.
The {{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|WD=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki}} mentions an event during the 5th year of {{ill|Emperor Richū|en|Emperor Richū|WD=Q329704|lt=Emperor Richū}} (404 CE, estimated). When Emperor Richū mourned the death of his consort, {{ill|Kurohime|ja|葛城黒媛|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kurohime|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, divine punishment was blamed on Kuramochi no Kimi. He had misused resources assigned to {{ill|Kuramochibe|ja|車持部|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuramochibe|qq=}} for investigations in {{ill|Tsukushi Province|en|Tsukushi Province|WD=Q1206461|lt=Tsukushi Province}} and confiscated goods dedicated to {{ill|Munakata Shrine|ja|宗像神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Munakata Shrine|1=Munakata shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11451876}}. As a result, the emperor condemned Kuramochi no Kimi, ordering purification rites at Nagasunozaki (now Nagasu, {{ill|Amagasaki|en|Amagasaki|WD=Q213318|lt=Amagasaki}}), and banned him from managing Kuramochibe in Tsukushi.<ref>''Nihon Shoki,'' Emperor Richū, 5th year, 10th month, 11th day entry.</ref>
The ''Nihon Shoki,'' citing the "Baekje Annals," records that in the 3rd year of {{ill|Emperor Keitai|en|Emperor Keitai|WD=Q349277|lt=Emperor Keitai}} (509), Kuramochi no Kimi (久羅麻致支弥, Kuramachikimi) was sent to [[:en:Baekje|Baekje]] to return refugees who had fled to {{ill|Mimana|en|Mimana|WD=Q1790223|lt=Mimana}} back to their homeland and re-register them.<ref>{{Cite web|title=久羅麻致支弥|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/久羅麻致支弥-1072490|publisher=Kotobank}}</ref>
During the {{ill|Asuka period|en|Asuka period|WD=Q210688|lt=Asuka period}}, Kuramochi Kunikko’s daughter, {{ill|Kuramochi Yoshikome|ja|車持与志古娘|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuramochi Yoshikome|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, married [[:en:Nakatomi no Kamatari|Nakatomi no Kamatari]], giving birth to {{ill|Fujiwara no Fuhito|en|Fujiwara no Fuhito|WD=Q177517|lt=Fujiwara no Fuhito}}.
In the {{ill|Nara period|en|Nara period|WD=Q189178|lt=Nara period}}, {{ill|Kuramochi Chitose|ja|車持千年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuramochi Chitose|qq=}} served as a court poet for {{ill|Empress Genmei|en|Empress Genmei|WD=Q233224|lt=Empress Genmei}} and {{ill|Emperor Shōmu|en|Emperor Shōmu|WD=Q349263|lt=Emperor Shōmu}}.
According to the {{ill|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|en|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|WD=Q1052981|lt=Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku}}, compiled in 882, the Kuramochi clan served under the {{ill|Traditional Chinese law|en|Traditional Chinese law|WD=Q4380197|lt=Ritsuryō}} system as subordinates in the {{ill|Ministry of the Imperial Household|en|Ministry of the Imperial Household|WD=Q8193428|lt=Ministry of the Imperial Household (Ritsuryō)}}’s {{ill|Shudenryō|ja|主殿寮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shudenryō|qq=}}. Their duties included managing imperial palanquins. The ''Engishiki'' mentions a member of the Kuramochi clan performing ceremonial roles during the {{ill|Daijosai|en|Daijosai|WD=Q11433653|lt=Daijosai}}.<ref>''Engishiki,'' vol. 7, ''Shinji,'' "Daijōsai," Article 40.</ref>
The ''Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku'' also states that in 877, the Kuramochi clan and five other clans—Kamitsukeno, Ōno, Ikeda, Sami, and Shimotsukeno—were all descendants of Emperor Sujin. According to the ''Shoku Nihongi,'' a petition submitted by {{ill|Sumiyoshi no Tsunamori|ja|住吉綱主|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sumiyoshi no Tsunamori|qq=}} in 791 noted that these clans were settled in the eastern provinces, taking surnames based on their places of residence. Archaeological evidence from wooden tablets excavated from the Fujiwara Palace indicates that Kamitsukeno’s territory included a district named "Kuramochi District" until the late 7th century.
The {{ill|Hodota Kofun Cluster|en|Hodota Kofun Cluster|WD=Q11385853|lt=Hodota Kofun Group}} in {{ill|Takasaki|en|Takasaki|WD=Q336438|lt=Takasaki}}, Gunma Prefecture, includes three keyhole-shaped Kofuns constructed between the late 5th and early 6th centuries. These are believed to be tombs of regional leaders linked to the formation of the Keno Province. The nearby {{ill|Mitsudera Site|ja|三ツ寺Ⅰ遺跡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mitsudera Site|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} further highlights connections between these clans and the Yamato court.
The Kuramochi clan’s {{ill|Kabane|ja|姓|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kabane|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} was originally "Kimi," later elevated to "Ason" under the {{ill|Eight Prince Ranks|ja|八色の姓|1=Eight Kabane system|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eight Prince Ranks|13=User:Immanuelle/Eight Kabane system|qq=}} reform initiated by {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|1=Tenmu|2=ja|3=天武天皇|ja|Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu|2=ja|3=天武天皇|1=Emperor Tenmu|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Tenmu|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Tenmu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q335181}}.<ref>''Nihon Shoki,'' Emperor Tenmu, 13th year, 11th month, 1st day entry.</ref>
== Kuramochi Clan in Wooden Tablets and Registers == * Kuramochi Ason (Name unknown), Kuramochi Ason Michimichi *: Names appear on wooden tablets excavated from the Dazaifu government complex. * Kuramochi no Kimi Nemaro, Kuramochi no Kimi Ikusa, Kuramochi no Kimi Hitsuji *: Appears in the fragmentary register "Bungo Province Nakatsu District Household Registry of 702." * Kuramochi no Sazumaro, Kuramochi no Takan *: Names appear on wooden tablets excavated from the Heijō Palace site. * Kuramochi no Wakamaru *: Appears on labels excavated from the Heijō Palace site.
== References == {{reflist}}
== Sources == * ''Nihon Shoki,'' Volumes 3 and 5, {{ill|Iwanami Bunko|ja|岩波文庫|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwanami Bunko|1=Iwanami Bunko|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11475322}}, 1994–1995. * {{ill|Ujitani Mitsuru|ja|宇治谷孟|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ujitani Mitsuru|qq=}}, ''Nihon Shoki: Complete Modern Translation,'' Vols. 1–2, {{ill|Kōdansha Gakujutsu Bunko|ja|講談社学術文庫|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōdansha Gakujutsu Bunko|1=Kōdansha gakujutsu bunko|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11632220}}, 1988. * {{ill|Shoku Nihongi|en|Shoku Nihongi|WD=Q611399|lt=Shoku Nihongi}} Volumes 1 and 5, {{ill|Iwanami Shoten|en|Iwanami Shoten|WD=Q1331427|lt=Iwanami Shoten}}, 1989, 1998. * {{ill|Saeki Ariyoshi|ja|佐伯有清|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saeki Ariyoshi|qq=}}, ''Japanese Ancient Clan Encyclopedia,'' 2015. * {{ill|Sakamoto Tarō|ja|坂本太郎|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakamoto Tarō|1=Sakamoto Tarō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11425251}}, ''Japanese Ancient Clan Names Dictionary,'' {{ill|Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|ja|吉川弘文館|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|1=Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4346755}}, 1990. * {{ill|Rizō Takeuchi|en|Rizō Takeuchi|WD=Q4262779|lt=Takeuchi Rizo}}, ''Japanese Ancient Names Dictionary,'' Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1972.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:車持氏]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:車持氏]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:車持氏]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q120336431]] Wikidata name: Kurumamochi clan [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:45, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Kuramochi Clan''' (車持氏) is a clan that takes its name, "Kuramochi," from its family lineage. This clan oversaw the occupational group ({{ill|Shinabe clans|en|Shinabe clans|WD=Q11418456|lt=Shinabe}}) known as the {{ill|Kuramochibe|ja|車持部|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuramochibe|qq=}}, which managed specific duties.
== Overview ==
According to the {{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|en|Shinsen Shōjiroku|WD=Q1045362|lt=Shinsen Shōjiroku}}, under the Left Capital section of imperial lineage, the Kuramochi clan shared the same ancestry as the {{ill|Kamitsukeno clan|ja|上毛野氏|1=Kamitsukeno clan|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamitsukeno clan|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamitsukeno clan|qq=}}. They were descendants of {{ill|Toyokiirihiko-no-mikoto|ja|豊城入彦命|1=Toyokiirihiko-no-Mikoto|1=Toyokiirihiko-no-Mikoto|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyokiirihiko-no-mikoto|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyokiirihiko-no-Mikoto|qq=}}, specifically from his eighth-generation descendant Isasa no Kimi. During the reign of {{ill|Emperor Yūryaku|en|Emperor Yūryaku|WD=Q357235|lt=Emperor Yūryaku}}, the clan earned the surname "Kuramochi no Kimi" after offering vehicles to the court.
The {{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|WD=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki}} mentions an event during the 5th year of {{ill|Emperor Richū|en|Emperor Richū|WD=Q329704|lt=Emperor Richū}} (404 CE, estimated). When Emperor Richū mourned the death of his consort, {{ill|Kurohime|ja|葛城黒媛|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kurohime|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, divine punishment was blamed on Kuramochi no Kimi. He had misused resources assigned to {{ill|Kuramochibe|ja|車持部|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuramochibe|qq=}} for investigations in {{ill|Tsukushi Province|en|Tsukushi Province|WD=Q1206461|lt=Tsukushi Province}} and confiscated goods dedicated to {{ill|Munakata Shrine|ja|宗像神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Munakata Shrine|1=Munakata shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11451876}}. As a result, the emperor condemned Kuramochi no Kimi, ordering purification rites at Nagasunozaki (now Nagasu, {{ill|Amagasaki|en|Amagasaki|WD=Q213318|lt=Amagasaki}}), and banned him from managing Kuramochibe in Tsukushi.<ref>''Nihon Shoki,'' Emperor Richū, 5th year, 10th month, 11th day entry.</ref>
The ''Nihon Shoki,'' citing the "Baekje Annals," records that in the 3rd year of {{ill|Emperor Keitai|en|Emperor Keitai|WD=Q349277|lt=Emperor Keitai}} (509), Kuramochi no Kimi (久羅麻致支弥, Kuramachikimi) was sent to [[:en:Baekje|Baekje]] to return refugees who had fled to {{ill|Mimana|en|Mimana|WD=Q1790223|lt=Mimana}} back to their homeland and re-register them.<ref>{{Cite web|title=久羅麻致支弥|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/久羅麻致支弥-1072490|publisher=Kotobank}}</ref>
During the {{ill|Asuka period|en|Asuka period|WD=Q210688|lt=Asuka period}}, Kuramochi Kunikko’s daughter, {{ill|Kuramochi Yoshikome|ja|車持与志古娘|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuramochi Yoshikome|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, married [[:en:Nakatomi no Kamatari|Nakatomi no Kamatari]], giving birth to {{ill|Fujiwara no Fuhito|en|Fujiwara no Fuhito|WD=Q177517|lt=Fujiwara no Fuhito}}.
In the {{ill|Nara period|en|Nara period|WD=Q189178|lt=Nara period}}, {{ill|Kuramochi Chitose|ja|車持千年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuramochi Chitose|qq=}} served as a court poet for {{ill|Empress Genmei|en|Empress Genmei|WD=Q233224|lt=Empress Genmei}} and {{ill|Emperor Shōmu|en|Emperor Shōmu|WD=Q349263|lt=Emperor Shōmu}}.
According to the {{ill|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|en|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|WD=Q1052981|lt=Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku}}, compiled in 882, the Kuramochi clan served under the {{ill|Traditional Chinese law|en|Traditional Chinese law|WD=Q4380197|lt=Ritsuryō}} system as subordinates in the {{ill|Ministry of the Imperial Household|en|Ministry of the Imperial Household|WD=Q8193428|lt=Ministry of the Imperial Household (Ritsuryō)}}’s {{ill|Shudenryō|ja|主殿寮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shudenryō|qq=}}. Their duties included managing imperial palanquins. The ''Engishiki'' mentions a member of the Kuramochi clan performing ceremonial roles during the {{ill|Daijosai|en|Daijosai|WD=Q11433653|lt=Daijosai}}.<ref>''Engishiki,'' vol. 7, ''Shinji,'' "Daijōsai," Article 40.</ref>
The ''Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku'' also states that in 877, the Kuramochi clan and five other clans—Kamitsukeno, Ōno, Ikeda, Sami, and Shimotsukeno—were all descendants of Emperor Sujin. According to the ''Shoku Nihongi,'' a petition submitted by {{ill|Sumiyoshi no Tsunamori|ja|住吉綱主|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sumiyoshi no Tsunamori|qq=}} in 791 noted that these clans were settled in the eastern provinces, taking surnames based on their places of residence. Archaeological evidence from wooden tablets excavated from the Fujiwara Palace indicates that Kamitsukeno’s territory included a district named "Kuramochi District" until the late 7th century.
The {{ill|Hodota Kofun Cluster|en|Hodota Kofun Cluster|WD=Q11385853|lt=Hodota Kofun Group}} in {{ill|Takasaki|en|Takasaki|WD=Q336438|lt=Takasaki}}, Gunma Prefecture, includes three keyhole-shaped Kofuns constructed between the late 5th and early 6th centuries. These are believed to be tombs of regional leaders linked to the formation of the Keno Province. The nearby {{ill|Mitsudera Site|ja|三ツ寺Ⅰ遺跡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mitsudera Site|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} further highlights connections between these clans and the Yamato court.
The Kuramochi clan’s {{ill|Kabane|ja|姓|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kabane|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} was originally "Kimi," later elevated to "Ason" under the {{ill|Eight Prince Ranks|ja|八色の姓|1=Eight Kabane system|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eight Prince Ranks|13=User:Immanuelle/Eight Kabane system|qq=}} reform initiated by {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|1=Tenmu|2=ja|3=天武天皇|ja|Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu|2=ja|3=天武天皇|1=Emperor Tenmu|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Tenmu|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Tenmu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q335181}}.<ref>''Nihon Shoki,'' Emperor Tenmu, 13th year, 11th month, 1st day entry.</ref>
== Kuramochi Clan in Wooden Tablets and Registers == * Kuramochi Ason (Name unknown), Kuramochi Ason Michimichi *: Names appear on wooden tablets excavated from the Dazaifu government complex. * Kuramochi no Kimi Nemaro, Kuramochi no Kimi Ikusa, Kuramochi no Kimi Hitsuji *: Appears in the fragmentary register "Bungo Province Nakatsu District Household Registry of 702." * Kuramochi no Sazumaro, Kuramochi no Takan *: Names appear on wooden tablets excavated from the Heijō Palace site. * Kuramochi no Wakamaru *: Appears on labels excavated from the Heijō Palace site.
== References == {{reflist}}
== Sources == * ''Nihon Shoki,'' Volumes 3 and 5, {{ill|Iwanami Bunko|ja|岩波文庫|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwanami Bunko|1=Iwanami Bunko|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11475322}}, 1994–1995. * {{ill|Ujitani Mitsuru|ja|宇治谷孟|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ujitani Mitsuru|qq=}}, ''Nihon Shoki: Complete Modern Translation,'' Vols. 1–2, {{ill|Kōdansha Gakujutsu Bunko|ja|講談社学術文庫|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōdansha Gakujutsu Bunko|1=Kōdansha gakujutsu bunko|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11632220}}, 1988. * {{ill|Shoku Nihongi|en|Shoku Nihongi|WD=Q611399|lt=Shoku Nihongi}} Volumes 1 and 5, {{ill|Iwanami Shoten|en|Iwanami Shoten|WD=Q1331427|lt=Iwanami Shoten}}, 1989, 1998. * {{ill|Saeki Ariyoshi|ja|佐伯有清|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saeki Ariyoshi|qq=}}, ''Japanese Ancient Clan Encyclopedia,'' 2015. * {{ill|Sakamoto Tarō|ja|坂本太郎|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakamoto Tarō|1=Sakamoto Tarō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11425251}}, ''Japanese Ancient Clan Names Dictionary,'' {{ill|Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|ja|吉川弘文館|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|1=Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4346755}}, 1990. * {{ill|Rizō Takeuchi|en|Rizō Takeuchi|WD=Q4262779|lt=Takeuchi Rizo}}, ''Japanese Ancient Names Dictionary,'' Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1972.

The {{ill|Hodota Kofun Cluster|en|Hodota Kofun Cluster|WD=Q11385853|lt=Hodota Kofun Group}} in {{ill|Takasaki|en|Takasaki|WD=Q336438|lt=Takasaki}}, Gunma Prefecture, includes three keyhole-shaped Kofuns constructed between the late 5th and early 6th centuries. These are believed to be tombs of regional leaders linked to the formation of the Keno Province. The nearby {{ill|Mitsudera Site|ja|三ツ寺Ⅰ遺跡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mitsudera Site|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} further highlights connections between these clans and the Yamato court.
The Kuramochi clan’s {{ill|Kabane|ja|姓|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kabane|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} was originally "Kimi," later elevated to "Ason" under the {{ill|Eight Prince Ranks|ja|八色の姓|1=Eight Kabane system|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eight Prince Ranks|13=User:Immanuelle/Eight Kabane system|qq=}} reform initiated by {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|1=Tenmu|2=ja|3=天武天皇|ja|Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu|2=ja|3=天武天皇|1=Emperor Tenmu|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Tenmu|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Tenmu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q335181}}.<ref>''Nihon Shoki,'' Emperor Tenmu, 13th year, 11th month, 1st day entry.</ref>
== Kuramochi Clan in Wooden Tablets and Registers == * Kuramochi Ason (Name unknown), Kuramochi Ason Michimichi *: Names appear on wooden tablets excavated from the Dazaifu government complex. * Kuramochi no Kimi Nemaro, Kuramochi no Kimi Ikusa, Kuramochi no Kimi Hitsuji *: Appears in the fragmentary register "Bungo Province Nakatsu District Household Registry of 702." * Kuramochi no Sazumaro, Kuramochi no Takan *: Names appear on wooden tablets excavated from the Heijō Palace site. * Kuramochi no Wakamaru *: Appears on labels excavated from the Heijō Palace site.
== References == {{reflist}}
== Sources == * ''Nihon Shoki,'' Volumes 3 and 5, {{ill|Iwanami Bunko|ja|岩波文庫|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwanami Bunko|1=Iwanami Bunko|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11475322}}, 1994–1995. * {{ill|Ujitani Mitsuru|ja|宇治谷孟|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ujitani Mitsuru|qq=}}, ''Nihon Shoki: Complete Modern Translation,'' Vols. 1–2, {{ill|Kōdansha Gakujutsu Bunko|ja|講談社学術文庫|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōdansha Gakujutsu Bunko|1=Kōdansha gakujutsu bunko|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11632220}}, 1988. * {{ill|Shoku Nihongi|en|Shoku Nihongi|WD=Q611399|lt=Shoku Nihongi}} Volumes 1 and 5, {{ill|Iwanami Shoten|en|Iwanami Shoten|WD=Q1331427|lt=Iwanami Shoten}}, 1989, 1998. * {{ill|Saeki Ariyoshi|ja|佐伯有清|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saeki Ariyoshi|qq=}}, ''Japanese Ancient Clan Encyclopedia,'' 2015. * {{ill|Sakamoto Tarō|ja|坂本太郎|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakamoto Tarō|1=Sakamoto Tarō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11425251}}, ''Japanese Ancient Clan Names Dictionary,'' {{ill|Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|ja|吉川弘文館|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|1=Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4346755}}, 1990. * {{ill|Rizō Takeuchi|en|Rizō Takeuchi|WD=Q4262779|lt=Takeuchi Rizo}}, ''Japanese Ancient Names Dictionary,'' Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1972.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:車持氏]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:車持氏]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:車持氏]]
'''Kuramochi Clan''' (車持氏) is a clan that takes its name, "Kuramochi," from its family lineage. This clan oversaw the occupational group ({{ill|Shinabe clans|en|Shinabe clans|WD=Q11418456|lt=Shinabe}}) known as the {{ill|Kuramochibe|ja|車持部|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuramochibe|qq=}}, which managed specific duties.
== Overview ==
According to the {{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|en|Shinsen Shōjiroku|WD=Q1045362|lt=Shinsen Shōjiroku}}, under the Left Capital section of imperial lineage, the Kuramochi clan shared the same ancestry as the {{ill|Kamitsukeno clan|ja|上毛野氏|1=Kamitsukeno clan|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamitsukeno clan|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamitsukeno clan|qq=}}. They were descendants of {{ill|Toyokiirihiko-no-mikoto|ja|豊城入彦命|1=Prince Toyokiirihiko|1=Prince Toyokiirihiko|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyokiirihiko-no-mikoto|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Toyokiirihiko|qq=}}, specifically from his eighth-generation descendant Isasa no Kimi. During the reign of {{ill|Emperor Yūryaku|en|Emperor Yūryaku|WD=Q357235|lt=Emperor Yūryaku}}, the clan earned the surname "Kuramochi no Kimi" after offering vehicles to the court.
The {{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|WD=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki}} mentions an event during the 5th year of {{ill|Emperor Richū|en|Emperor Richū|WD=Q329704|lt=Emperor Richū}} (404 CE, estimated). When Emperor Richū mourned the death of his consort, {{ill|Kurohime|ja|葛城黒媛|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kurohime|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, divine punishment was blamed on Kuramochi no Kimi. He had misused resources assigned to {{ill|Kuramochibe|ja|車持部|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuramochibe|qq=}} for investigations in {{ill|Tsukushi Province|en|Tsukushi Province|WD=Q1206461|lt=Tsukushi Province}} and confiscated goods dedicated to {{ill|Munakata Shrine|ja|宗像神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Munakata Shrine|1=Munakata shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11451876}}. As a result, the emperor condemned Kuramochi no Kimi, ordering purification rites at Nagasunozaki (now Nagasu, {{ill|Amagasaki|en|Amagasaki|WD=Q213318|lt=Amagasaki}}), and banned him from managing Kuramochibe in Tsukushi.<ref>''Nihon Shoki,'' Emperor Richū, 5th year, 10th month, 11th day entry.</ref>
The ''Nihon Shoki,'' citing the "Baekje Annals," records that in the 3rd year of {{ill|Emperor Keitai|en|Emperor Keitai|WD=Q349277|lt=Emperor Keitai}} (509), Kuramochi no Kimi (久羅麻致支弥, Kuramachikimi) was sent to [[:en:Baekje|Baekje]] to return refugees who had fled to {{ill|Mimana|en|Mimana|WD=Q1790223|lt=Mimana}} back to their homeland and re-register them.<ref>{{Cite web|title=久羅麻致支弥|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/久羅麻致支弥-1072490|publisher=Kotobank}}</ref>
During the {{ill|Asuka period|en|Asuka period|WD=Q210688|lt=Asuka period}}, Kuramochi Kunikko’s daughter, {{ill|Kuramochi Yoshikome|ja|車持与志古娘|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuramochi Yoshikome|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, married [[:en:Nakatomi no Kamatari|Nakatomi no Kamatari]], giving birth to {{ill|Fujiwara no Fuhito|en|Fujiwara no Fuhito|WD=Q177517|lt=Fujiwara no Fuhito}}.
In the {{ill|Nara period|en|Nara period|WD=Q189178|lt=Nara period}}, {{ill|Kuramochi Chitose|ja|車持千年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuramochi Chitose|qq=}} served as a court poet for {{ill|Empress Genmei|en|Empress Genmei|WD=Q233224|lt=Empress Genmei}} and {{ill|Emperor Shōmu|en|Emperor Shōmu|WD=Q349263|lt=Emperor Shōmu}}.
According to the {{ill|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|en|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|WD=Q1052981|lt=Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku}}, compiled in 882, the Kuramochi clan served under the {{ill|Traditional Chinese law|en|Traditional Chinese law|WD=Q4380197|lt=Ritsuryō}} system as subordinates in the {{ill|Ministry of the Imperial Household|en|Ministry of the Imperial Household|WD=Q8193428|lt=Ministry of the Imperial Household (Ritsuryō)}}’s {{ill|Shudenryō|ja|主殿寮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shudenryō|qq=}}. Their duties included managing imperial palanquins. The ''Engishiki'' mentions a member of the Kuramochi clan performing ceremonial roles during the {{ill|Daijosai|en|Daijosai|WD=Q11433653|lt=Daijosai}}.<ref>''Engishiki,'' vol. 7, ''Shinji,'' "Daijōsai," Article 40.</ref>
The ''Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku'' also states that in 877, the Kuramochi clan and five other clans—Kamitsukeno, Ōno, Ikeda, Sami, and Shimotsukeno—were all descendants of Emperor Sujin. According to the ''Shoku Nihongi,'' a petition submitted by {{ill|Sumiyoshi no Tsunamori|ja|住吉綱主|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sumiyoshi no Tsunamori|qq=}} in 791 noted that these clans were settled in the eastern provinces, taking surnames based on their places of residence. Archaeological evidence from wooden tablets excavated from the Fujiwara Palace indicates that Kamitsukeno’s territory included a district named "Kuramochi District" until the late 7th century.
The {{ill|Hodota Kofun Cluster|en|Hodota Kofun Cluster|WD=Q11385853|lt=Hodota Kofun Group}} in {{ill|Takasaki|en|Takasaki|WD=Q336438|lt=Takasaki}}, Gunma Prefecture, includes three keyhole-shaped Kofuns constructed between the late 5th and early 6th centuries. These are believed to be tombs of regional leaders linked to the formation of the Keno Province. The nearby {{ill|Mitsudera Site|ja|三ツ寺Ⅰ遺跡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mitsudera Site|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} further highlights connections between these clans and the Yamato court.
The Kuramochi clan’s {{ill|Kabane|ja|姓|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kabane|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} was originally "Kimi," later elevated to "Ason" under the {{ill|Eight Prince Ranks|ja|八色の姓|1=Eight Kabane system|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eight Prince Ranks|13=User:Immanuelle/Eight Kabane system|qq=}} reform initiated by {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|1=Tenmu|2=ja|3=天武天皇|ja|Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu|2=ja|3=天武天皇|1=Emperor Tenmu|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Tenmu|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Tenmu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q335181}}.<ref>''Nihon Shoki,'' Emperor Tenmu, 13th year, 11th month, 1st day entry.</ref>
== Kuramochi Clan in Wooden Tablets and Registers == * Kuramochi Ason (Name unknown), Kuramochi Ason Michimichi *: Names appear on wooden tablets excavated from the Dazaifu government complex. * Kuramochi no Kimi Nemaro, Kuramochi no Kimi Ikusa, Kuramochi no Kimi Hitsuji *: Appears in the fragmentary register "Bungo Province Nakatsu District Household Registry of 702." * Kuramochi no Sazumaro, Kuramochi no Takan *: Names appear on wooden tablets excavated from the Heijō Palace site. * Kuramochi no Wakamaru *: Appears on labels excavated from the Heijō Palace site.
== References == {{reflist}}
== Sources == * ''Nihon Shoki,'' Volumes 3 and 5, {{ill|Iwanami Bunko|ja|岩波文庫|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwanami Bunko|1=Iwanami Bunko|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11475322}}, 1994–1995. * {{ill|Ujitani Mitsuru|ja|宇治谷孟|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ujitani Mitsuru|qq=}}, ''Nihon Shoki: Complete Modern Translation,'' Vols. 1–2, {{ill|Kōdansha Gakujutsu Bunko|ja|講談社学術文庫|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōdansha Gakujutsu Bunko|1=Kōdansha gakujutsu bunko|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11632220}}, 1988. * {{ill|Shoku Nihongi|en|Shoku Nihongi|WD=Q611399|lt=Shoku Nihongi}} Volumes 1 and 5, {{ill|Iwanami Shoten|en|Iwanami Shoten|WD=Q1331427|lt=Iwanami Shoten}}, 1989, 1998. * {{ill|Saeki Ariyoshi|ja|佐伯有清|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saeki Ariyoshi|qq=}}, ''Japanese Ancient Clan Encyclopedia,'' 2015. * {{ill|Sakamoto Tarō|ja|坂本太郎|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakamoto Tarō|1=Sakamoto Tarō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11425251}}, ''Japanese Ancient Clan Names Dictionary,'' {{ill|Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|ja|吉川弘文館|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|1=Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4346755}}, 1990. * {{ill|Rizō Takeuchi|en|Rizō Takeuchi|WD=Q4262779|lt=Takeuchi Rizo}}, ''Japanese Ancient Names Dictionary,'' Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1972.

The {{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|WD=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki}} mentions an event during the 5th year of {{ill|Emperor Richū|en|Emperor Richū|WD=Q329704|lt=Emperor Richū}} (404 CE, estimated). <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:車持氏]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:車持氏]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:車持氏]]
'''Kuramochi Clan''' (車持氏) is a clan that takes its name, "Kuramochi," from its family lineage. This clan oversaw the occupational group ({{ill|Shinabe clans|en|Shinabe clans|WD=Q11418456|lt=Shinabe}}) known as the {{ill|Kuramochibe|ja|車持部|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuramochibe|qq=}}, which managed specific duties.
== Overview ==
According to the {{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|en|Shinsen Shōjiroku|WD=Q1045362|lt=Shinsen Shōjiroku}}, under the Left Capital section of imperial lineage, the Kuramochi clan shared the same ancestry as the {{ill|Kamitsukeno clan|ja|上毛野氏|1=Kamitsukeno clan|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamitsukeno clan|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamitsukeno clan|qq=}}. They were descendants of {{ill|Toyokiirihiko-no-mikoto|ja|豊城入彦命|1=Prince Toyokiirihiko|2=ja|3=豊城入彦命|WD=Q11633375|1=Prince Toyokiirihiko|1=Prince Toyokiirihiko|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyokiirihiko-no-mikoto|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Toyokiirihiko|qq=}}, specifically from his eighth-generation descendant Isasa no Kimi. During the reign of {{ill|Emperor Yūryaku|en|Emperor Yūryaku|WD=Q357235|lt=Emperor Yūryaku}}, the clan earned the surname "Kuramochi no Kimi" after offering vehicles to the court.
The {{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|WD=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki}} mentions an event during the 5th year of {{ill|Emperor Richū|en|Emperor Richū|WD=Q329704|lt=Emperor Richū}} (404 CE, estimated). When Emperor Richū mourned the death of his consort, {{ill|Kurohime|ja|葛城黒媛|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kurohime|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, divine punishment was blamed on Kuramochi no Kimi. He had misused resources assigned to {{ill|Kuramochibe|ja|車持部|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuramochibe|qq=}} for investigations in {{ill|Tsukushi Province|en|Tsukushi Province|WD=Q1206461|lt=Tsukushi Province}} and confiscated goods dedicated to {{ill|Munakata Shrine|ja|宗像神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Munakata Shrine|1=Munakata shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11451876}}. As a result, the emperor condemned Kuramochi no Kimi, ordering purification rites at Nagasunozaki (now Nagasu, {{ill|Amagasaki|en|Amagasaki|WD=Q213318|lt=Amagasaki}}), and banned him from managing Kuramochibe in Tsukushi.<ref>''Nihon Shoki,'' Emperor Richū, 5th year, 10th month, 11th day entry.</ref>
The ''Nihon Shoki,'' citing the "Baekje Annals," records that in the 3rd year of {{ill|Emperor Keitai|en|Emperor Keitai|WD=Q349277|lt=Emperor Keitai}} (509), Kuramochi no Kimi (久羅麻致支弥, Kuramachikimi) was sent to [[:en:Baekje|Baekje]] to return refugees who had fled to {{ill|Mimana|en|Mimana|WD=Q1790223|lt=Mimana}} back to their homeland and re-register them.<ref>{{Cite web|title=久羅麻致支弥|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/久羅麻致支弥-1072490|publisher=Kotobank}}</ref>
During the {{ill|Asuka period|en|Asuka period|WD=Q210688|lt=Asuka period}}, Kuramochi Kunikko’s daughter, {{ill|Kuramochi Yoshikome|ja|車持与志古娘|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuramochi Yoshikome|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, married [[:en:Nakatomi no Kamatari|Nakatomi no Kamatari]], giving birth to {{ill|Fujiwara no Fuhito|en|Fujiwara no Fuhito|WD=Q177517|lt=Fujiwara no Fuhito}}.
In the {{ill|Nara period|en|Nara period|WD=Q189178|lt=Nara period}}, {{ill|Kuramochi Chitose|ja|車持千年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuramochi Chitose|qq=}} served as a court poet for {{ill|Empress Genmei|en|Empress Genmei|WD=Q233224|lt=Empress Genmei}} and {{ill|Emperor Shōmu|en|Emperor Shōmu|WD=Q349263|lt=Emperor Shōmu}}.
According to the {{ill|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|en|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|WD=Q1052981|lt=Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku}}, compiled in 882, the Kuramochi clan served under the {{ill|Traditional Chinese law|en|Traditional Chinese law|WD=Q4380197|lt=Ritsuryō}} system as subordinates in the {{ill|Ministry of the Imperial Household|en|Ministry of the Imperial Household|WD=Q8193428|lt=Ministry of the Imperial Household (Ritsuryō)}}’s {{ill|Shudenryō|ja|主殿寮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shudenryō|qq=}}. Their duties included managing imperial palanquins. The ''Engishiki'' mentions a member of the Kuramochi clan performing ceremonial roles during the {{ill|Daijosai|en|Daijosai|WD=Q11433653|lt=Daijosai}}.<ref>''Engishiki,'' vol. 7, ''Shinji,'' "Daijōsai," Article 40.</ref>
The ''Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku'' also states that in 877, the Kuramochi clan and five other clans—Kamitsukeno, Ōno, Ikeda, Sami, and Shimotsukeno—were all descendants of Emperor Sujin. According to the ''Shoku Nihongi,'' a petition submitted by {{ill|Sumiyoshi no Tsunamori|ja|住吉綱主|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sumiyoshi no Tsunamori|qq=}} in 791 noted that these clans were settled in the eastern provinces, taking surnames based on their places of residence. Archaeological evidence from wooden tablets excavated from the Fujiwara Palace indicates that Kamitsukeno’s territory included a district named "Kuramochi District" until the late 7th century.
The {{ill|Hodota Kofun Cluster|en|Hodota Kofun Cluster|WD=Q11385853|lt=Hodota Kofun Group}} in {{ill|Takasaki|en|Takasaki|WD=Q336438|lt=Takasaki}}, Gunma Prefecture, includes three keyhole-shaped Kofuns constructed between the late 5th and early 6th centuries. These are believed to be tombs of regional leaders linked to the formation of the Keno Province. The nearby {{ill|Mitsudera Site|ja|三ツ寺Ⅰ遺跡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mitsudera Site|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} further highlights connections between these clans and the Yamato court.
The Kuramochi clan’s {{ill|Kabane|ja|姓|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kabane|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} was originally "Kimi," later elevated to "Ason" under the {{ill|Eight Prince Ranks|ja|八色の姓|1=Eight Kabane system|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eight Prince Ranks|13=User:Immanuelle/Eight Kabane system|qq=}} reform initiated by {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|1=Tenmu|2=ja|3=天武天皇|ja|Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu|2=ja|3=天武天皇|1=Emperor Tenmu|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Tenmu|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Tenmu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q335181}}.<ref>''Nihon Shoki,'' Emperor Tenmu, 13th year, 11th month, 1st day entry.</ref>
== Kuramochi Clan in Wooden Tablets and Registers == * Kuramochi Ason (Name unknown), Kuramochi Ason Michimichi *: Names appear on wooden tablets excavated from the Dazaifu government complex. * Kuramochi no Kimi Nemaro, Kuramochi no Kimi Ikusa, Kuramochi no Kimi Hitsuji *: Appears in the fragmentary register "Bungo Province Nakatsu District Household Registry of 702." * Kuramochi no Sazumaro, Kuramochi no Takan *: Names appear on wooden tablets excavated from the Heijō Palace site. * Kuramochi no Wakamaru *: Appears on labels excavated from the Heijō Palace site.
== References == {{reflist}}
== Sources == * ''Nihon Shoki,'' Volumes 3 and 5, {{ill|Iwanami Bunko|ja|岩波文庫|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwanami Bunko|1=Iwanami Bunko|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11475322}}, 1994–1995. * {{ill|Ujitani Mitsuru|ja|宇治谷孟|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ujitani Mitsuru|qq=}}, ''Nihon Shoki: Complete Modern Translation,'' Vols. 1–2, {{ill|Kōdansha Gakujutsu Bunko|ja|講談社学術文庫|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōdansha Gakujutsu Bunko|1=Kōdansha gakujutsu bunko|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11632220}}, 1988. * {{ill|Shoku Nihongi|en|Shoku Nihongi|WD=Q611399|lt=Shoku Nihongi}} Volumes 1 and 5, {{ill|Iwanami Shoten|en|Iwanami Shoten|WD=Q1331427|lt=Iwanami Shoten}}, 1989, 1998. * {{ill|Saeki Ariyoshi|ja|佐伯有清|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saeki Ariyoshi|qq=}}, ''Japanese Ancient Clan Encyclopedia,'' 2015. * {{ill|Sakamoto Tarō|ja|坂本太郎|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakamoto Tarō|1=Sakamoto Tarō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11425251}}, ''Japanese Ancient Clan Names Dictionary,'' {{ill|Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|ja|吉川弘文館|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|1=Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4346755}}, 1990. * {{ill|Rizō Takeuchi|en|Rizō Takeuchi|WD=Q4262779|lt=Takeuchi Rizo}}, ''Japanese Ancient Names Dictionary,'' Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1972.
{{Japanese-history-stub}} {{people-stub}}

The Kuramochi clan’s {{ill|Kabane|ja|姓|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kabane|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} was originally "Kimi," later elevated to "Ason" under the {{ill|Eight Prince Ranks|ja|八色の姓|1=Eight Kabane system|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eight Prince Ranks|13=User:Immanuelle/Eight Kabane system|qq=}} reform initiated by {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|1=Tenmu|2=ja|3=天武天皇|ja|Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu|2=ja|3=天武天皇|1=Emperor Tenmu|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Tenmu|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Tenmu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q335181}}.<ref>''Nihon Shoki,'' Emperor Tenmu, 13th year, 11th month, 1st day entry.</ref>
== Kuramochi Clan in Wooden Tablets and Registers == * Kuramochi Ason (Name unknown), Kuramochi Ason Michimichi *: Names appear on wooden tablets excavated from the Dazaifu government complex. * Kuramochi no Kimi Nemaro, Kuramochi no Kimi Ikusa, Kuramochi no Kimi Hitsuji *: Appears in the fragmentary register "Bungo Province Nakatsu District Household Registry of 702." * Kuramochi no Sazumaro, Kuramochi no Takan *: Names appear on wooden tablets excavated from the Heijō Palace site. * Kuramochi no Wakamaru *: Appears on labels excavated from the Heijō Palace site.
== References == {{reflist}}
== Sources == * ''Nihon Shoki,'' Volumes 3 and 5, {{ill|Iwanami Bunko|ja|岩波文庫|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwanami Bunko|1=Iwanami Bunko|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11475322}}, 1994–1995. * {{ill|Ujitani Mitsuru|ja|宇治谷孟|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ujitani Mitsuru|qq=}}, ''Nihon Shoki: Complete Modern Translation,'' Vols. 1–2, {{ill|Kōdansha Gakujutsu Bunko|ja|講談社学術文庫|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōdansha Gakujutsu Bunko|1=Kōdansha gakujutsu bunko|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11632220}}, 1988. * {{ill|Shoku Nihongi|en|Shoku Nihongi|WD=Q611399|lt=Shoku Nihongi}} Volumes 1 and 5, {{ill|Iwanami Shoten|en|Iwanami Shoten|WD=Q1331427|lt=Iwanami Shoten}}, 1989, 1998. * {{ill|Saeki Ariyoshi|ja|佐伯有清|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saeki Ariyoshi|qq=}}, ''Japanese Ancient Clan Encyclopedia,'' 2015. * {{ill|Sakamoto Tarō|ja|坂本太郎|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakamoto Tarō|1=Sakamoto Tarō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11425251}}, ''Japanese Ancient Clan Names Dictionary,'' {{ill|Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|ja|吉川弘文館|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|1=Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4346755}}, 1990. * {{ill|Rizō Takeuchi|en|Rizō Takeuchi|WD=Q4262779|lt=Takeuchi Rizo}}, ''Japanese Ancient Names Dictionary,'' Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1972.

He had misused resources assigned to {{ill|Kuramochibe|ja|車持部|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuramochibe|qq=}} for investigations in {{ill|Tsukushi Province|en|Tsukushi Province|WD=Q1206461|lt=Tsukushi Province|1=Tsukushi Province}} and confiscated goods dedicated to {{ill|Munakata Shrine|ja|宗像神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Munakata Shrine|1=Munakata Shrines|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11451876}}. As a result, the emperor condemned Kuramochi no Kimi, ordering purification rites at Nagasunozaki (now Nagasu, {{ill|Amagasaki|en|Amagasaki|WD=Q213318|lt=Amagasaki|1=Amagasaki}}), and banned him from managing Kuramochibe in Tsukushi.<ref>''Nihon Shoki,'' Emperor Richū, 5th year, 10th month, 11th day entry.</ref>
The ''Nihon Shoki,'' citing the "Baekje Annals," records that in the 3rd year of {{ill|Emperor Keitai|en|Emperor Keitai|WD=Q349277|lt=Emperor Keitai|1=Emperor Keitai}} (509), Kuramochi no Kimi (久羅麻致支弥, Kuramachikimi) was sent to [[:en:Baekje|Baekje]] to return refugees who had fled to {{ill|Mimana|en|Mimana|WD=Q1790223|lt=Mimana|1=Mimana}} back to their homeland and re-register them.<ref>{{Cite web|title=久羅麻致支弥|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/久羅麻致支弥-1072490|publisher=Kotobank}}</ref>
During the {{ill|Asuka period|en|Asuka period|WD=Q210688|lt=Asuka period|1=Asuka period}}, Kuramochi Kunikko’s daughter, {{ill|Kuramochi Yoshikome|ja|車持与志古娘|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuramochi Yoshikome|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, married [[:en:Nakatomi no Kamatari|Nakatomi no Kamatari]], giving birth to {{ill|Fujiwara no Fuhito|en|Fujiwara no Fuhito|WD=Q177517|lt=Fujiwara no Fuhito|1=Fujiwara no Fuhito}}.
In the {{ill|Nara period|en|Nara period|WD=Q189178|lt=Nara period|1=Nara period}}, {{ill|Kuramochi Chitose|ja|車持千年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuramochi Chitose|qq=}} served as a court poet for {{ill|Empress Genmei|en|Empress Genmei|WD=Q233224|lt=Empress Genmei|1=Empress Genmei}} and {{ill|Emperor Shōmu|en|Emperor Shōmu|WD=Q349263|lt=Emperor Shōmu|1=Emperor Shōmu}}.
According to the {{ill|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|en|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|WD=Q1052981|lt=Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|1=Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku}}, compiled in 882, the Kuramochi clan served under the {{ill|Traditional Chinese law|en|Traditional Chinese law|WD=Q4380197|lt=Ritsuryō|1=Traditional Chinese law}} system as subordinates in the {{ill|Ministry of the Imperial Household|en|Ministry of the Imperial Household|WD=Q8193428|lt=Ministry of the Imperial Household (Ritsuryō)|1=Ministry of the Imperial Household}}’s {{ill|Shudenryō|ja|主殿寮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shudenryō|qq=}}. Their duties included managing imperial palanquins. The ''Engishiki'' mentions a member of the Kuramochi clan performing ceremonial roles during the {{ill|Daijosai|en|Daijosai|WD=Q11433653|lt=Daijosai|1=Daijosai}}.<ref>''Engishiki,'' vol. 7, ''Shinji,'' "Daijōsai," Article 40.</ref>
The ''Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku'' also states that in 877, the Kuramochi clan and five other clans—Kamitsukeno, Ōno, Ikeda, Sami, and Shimotsukeno—were all descendants of Emperor Sujin. According to the ''Shoku Nihongi,'' a petition submitted by {{ill|Sumiyoshi no Tsunamori|ja|住吉綱主|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sumiyoshi no Tsunamori|qq=}} in 791 noted that these clans were settled in the eastern provinces, taking surnames based on their places of residence. Archaeological evidence from wooden tablets excavated from the Fujiwara Palace indicates that Kamitsukeno’s territory included a district named "Kuramochi District" until the late 7th century.
The {{ill|Hodota Kofun Cluster|en|Hodota Kofun Cluster|WD=Q11385853|lt=Hodota Kofun Group|1=Hodota Kofun Cluster}} in {{ill|Takasaki|en|Takasaki|WD=Q336438|lt=Takasaki|1=Takasaki}}, Gunma Prefecture, includes three keyhole-shaped Kofuns constructed between the late 5th and early 6th centuries. These are believed to be tombs of regional leaders linked to the formation of the Keno Province. The nearby {{ill|Mitsudera Site|ja|三ツ寺Ⅰ遺跡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mitsudera Site|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} further highlights connections between these clans and the Yamato court.
The Kuramochi clan’s {{ill|Kabane|ja|姓|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kabane|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} was originally "Kimi," later elevated to "Ason" under the {{ill|Eight Prince Ranks|ja|八色の姓|1=Eight Kabane system|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eight Prince Ranks|13=User:Immanuelle/Eight Kabane system|qq=}} reform initiated by {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu|2=ja|3=天武天皇|ja|Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu|2=ja|3=天武天皇|1=Emperor Tenmu|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Tenmu|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Tenmu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q335181}}.<ref>''Nihon Shoki,'' Emperor Tenmu, 13th year, 11th month, 1st day entry.</ref>
== Kuramochi Clan in Wooden Tablets and Registers == * Kuramochi Ason (Name unknown), Kuramochi Ason Michimichi *: Names appear on wooden tablets excavated from the Dazaifu government complex. * Kuramochi no Kimi Nemaro, Kuramochi no Kimi Ikusa, Kuramochi no Kimi Hitsuji *: Appears in the fragmentary register "Bungo Province Nakatsu District Household Registry of 702." * Kuramochi no Sazumaro, Kuramochi no Takan *: Names appear on wooden tablets excavated from the Heijō Palace site. * Kuramochi no Wakamaru *: Appears on labels excavated from the Heijō Palace site.

'''Kuramochi Clan''' (車持氏) is a clan that takes its name, "Kuramochi," from its family lineage. This clan oversaw the occupational group ({{ill|Shinabe clans|en|Shinabe clans|WD=Q11418456|lt=Shinabe|1=Shinabe clans}}) known as the {{ill|Kuramochibe|ja|車持部|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuramochibe|qq=}}, which managed specific duties.
== Overview ==
According to the {{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|en|Shinsen Shōjiroku|WD=Q1045362|lt=Shinsen Shōjiroku|1=Shinsen Shōjiroku}}, under the Left Capital section of imperial lineage, the Kuramochi clan shared the same ancestry as the {{ill|Kamitsukeno clan|ja|上毛野氏|1=Kamitsukeno clan|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamitsukeno clan|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamitsukeno clan|qq=}}. They were descendants of {{ill|Toyokiirihiko-no-mikoto|ja|豊城入彦命|1=Prince Toyokiirihiko|2=ja|3=豊城入彦命|WD=Q11633375|1=Prince Toyokiirihiko|1=Prince Toyokiirihiko|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyokiirihiko-no-mikoto|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Toyokiirihiko|qq=}}, specifically from his eighth-generation descendant Isasa no Kimi. During the reign of {{ill|Emperor Yūryaku|en|Emperor Yūryaku|WD=Q357235|lt=Emperor Yūryaku|1=Emperor Yūryaku}}, the clan earned the surname "Kuramochi no Kimi" after offering vehicles to the court.
The {{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|WD=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki|1=Nihon Shoki}} mentions an event during the 5th year of {{ill|Emperor Richū|en|Emperor Richū|WD=Q329704|lt=Emperor Richū|1=Emperor Richū}} (404 CE, estimated). When Emperor Richū mourned the death of his consort, {{ill|Kurohime|ja|葛城黒媛|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kurohime|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, divine punishment was blamed on Kuramochi no Kimi. He had misused resources assigned to {{ill|Kuramochibe|ja|車持部|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuramochibe|qq=}} for investigations in {{ill|Tsukushi Province|en|Tsukushi Province|WD=Q1206461|lt=Tsukushi Province|1=Tsukushi Province}} and confiscated goods dedicated to {{ill|Munakata Shrine|ja|宗像神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Munakata Shrine|1=Munakata Shrines|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11451876}}. As a result, the emperor condemned Kuramochi no Kimi, ordering purification rites at Nagasunozaki (now Nagasu, {{ill|Amagasaki|en|Amagasaki|WD=Q213318|lt=Amagasaki|1=Amagasaki}}), and banned him from managing Kuramochibe in Tsukushi.<ref>''Nihon Shoki,'' Emperor Richū, 5th year, 10th month, 11th day entry.</ref>
The ''Nihon Shoki,'' citing the "Baekje Annals," records that in the 3rd year of {{ill|Emperor Keitai|en|Emperor Keitai|WD=Q349277|lt=Emperor Keitai|1=Emperor Keitai}} (509), Kuramochi no Kimi (久羅麻致支弥, Kuramachikimi) was sent to [[:en:Baekje|Baekje]] to return refugees who had fled to {{ill|Mimana|en|Mimana|WD=Q1790223|lt=Mimana|1=Mimana}} back to their homeland and re-register them.<ref>{{Cite web|title=久羅麻致支弥|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/久羅麻致支弥-1072490|publisher=Kotobank}}</ref>
During the {{ill|Asuka period|en|Asuka period|WD=Q210688|lt=Asuka period|1=Asuka period}}, Kuramochi Kunikko’s daughter, {{ill|Kuramochi Yoshikome|ja|車持与志古娘|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuramochi Yoshikome|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, married [[:en:Nakatomi no Kamatari|Nakatomi no Kamatari]], giving birth to {{ill|Fujiwara no Fuhito|en|Fujiwara no Fuhito|WD=Q177517|lt=Fujiwara no Fuhito|1=Fujiwara no Fuhito}}.
In the {{ill|Nara period|en|Nara period|WD=Q189178|lt=Nara period|1=Nara period}}, {{ill|Kuramochi Chitose|ja|車持千年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuramochi Chitose|qq=}} served as a court poet for {{ill|Empress Genmei|en|Empress Genmei|WD=Q233224|lt=Empress Genmei|1=Empress Genmei}} and {{ill|Emperor Shōmu|en|Emperor Shōmu|WD=Q349263|lt=Emperor Shōmu|1=Emperor Shōmu}}.
According to the {{ill|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|en|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|WD=Q1052981|lt=Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|1=Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku}}, compiled in 882, the Kuramochi clan served under the {{ill|Traditional Chinese law|en|Traditional Chinese law|WD=Q4380197|lt=Ritsuryō|1=Traditional Chinese law}} system as subordinates in the {{ill|Ministry of the Imperial Household|en|Ministry of the Imperial Household|WD=Q8193428|lt=Ministry of the Imperial Household (Ritsuryō)|1=Ministry of the Imperial Household}}’s {{ill|Shudenryō|ja|主殿寮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shudenryō|qq=}}. Their duties included managing imperial palanquins. The ''Engishiki'' mentions a member of the Kuramochi clan performing ceremonial roles during the {{ill|Daijosai|en|Daijosai|WD=Q11433653|lt=Daijosai|1=Daijosai}}.<ref>''Engishiki,'' vol. 7, ''Shinji,'' "Daijōsai," Article 40.</ref>
The ''Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku'' also states that in 877, the Kuramochi clan and five other clans—Kamitsukeno, Ōno, Ikeda, Sami, and Shimotsukeno—were all descendants of Emperor Sujin. According to the ''Shoku Nihongi,'' a petition submitted by {{ill|Sumiyoshi no Tsunamori|ja|住吉綱主|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sumiyoshi no Tsunamori|qq=}} in 791 noted that these clans were settled in the eastern provinces, taking surnames based on their places of residence. Archaeological evidence from wooden tablets excavated from the Fujiwara Palace indicates that Kamitsukeno’s territory included a district named "Kuramochi District" until the late 7th century.
The {{ill|Hodota Kofun Cluster|en|Hodota Kofun Cluster|WD=Q11385853|lt=Hodota Kofun Group|1=Hodota Kofun Cluster}} in {{ill|Takasaki|en|Takasaki|WD=Q336438|lt=Takasaki|1=Takasaki}}, Gunma Prefecture, includes three keyhole-shaped Kofuns constructed between the late 5th and early 6th centuries. These are believed to be tombs of regional leaders linked to the formation of the Keno Province. The nearby {{ill|Mitsudera Site|ja|三ツ寺Ⅰ遺跡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mitsudera Site|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} further highlights connections between these clans and the Yamato court.
The Kuramochi clan’s {{ill|Kabane|ja|姓|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kabane|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} was originally "Kimi," later elevated to "Ason" under the {{ill|Eight Prince Ranks|ja|八色の姓|1=Eight Kabane system|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eight Prince Ranks|13=User:Immanuelle/Eight Kabane system|qq=}} reform initiated by {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu|2=ja|3=天武天皇|ja|Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu|2=ja|3=天武天皇|1=Emperor Tenmu|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Tenmu|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Tenmu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q335181}}.<ref>''Nihon Shoki,'' Emperor Tenmu, 13th year, 11th month, 1st day entry.</ref>
== Kuramochi Clan in Wooden Tablets and Registers == * Kuramochi Ason (Name unknown), Kuramochi Ason Michimichi *: Names appear on wooden tablets excavated from the Dazaifu government complex. * Kuramochi no Kimi Nemaro, Kuramochi no Kimi Ikusa, Kuramochi no Kimi Hitsuji *: Appears in the fragmentary register "Bungo Province Nakatsu District Household Registry of 702." * Kuramochi no Sazumaro, Kuramochi no Takan *: Names appear on wooden tablets excavated from the Heijō Palace site. * Kuramochi no Wakamaru *: Appears on labels excavated from the Heijō Palace site.

The ''Engishiki'' mentions a member of the Kuramochi clan performing ceremonial roles during the {{ill|Daijosai|en|Daijosai|WD=Q11433653|lt=Daijosai|1=Daijosai}}.<ref>''Engishiki,'' vol. 7, ''Shinji,'' "Daijōsai," Article 40.</ref>
The ''Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku'' also states that in 877, the Kuramochi clan and five other clans—Kamitsukeno, Ōno, Ikeda, Sami, and Shimotsukeno—were all descendants of Emperor Sujin. According to the ''Shoku Nihongi,'' a petition submitted by {{ill|Sumiyoshi no Tsunamori|ja|住吉綱主|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sumiyoshi no Tsunamori|qq=}} in 791 noted that these clans were settled in the eastern provinces, taking surnames based on their places of residence. Archaeological evidence from wooden tablets excavated from the Fujiwara Palace indicates that Kamitsukeno’s territory included a district named "Kuramochi District" until the late 7th century.
The {{ill|Hodota Kofun Cluster|en|Hodota Kofun Cluster|WD=Q11385853|lt=Hodota Kofun Group|1=Hodota Kofun Cluster}} in {{ill|Takasaki|en|Takasaki|WD=Q336438|lt=Takasaki|1=Takasaki}}, Gunma Prefecture, includes three keyhole-shaped Kofuns constructed between the late 5th and early 6th centuries. These are believed to be tombs of regional leaders linked to the formation of the Keno Province. The nearby {{ill|Mitsudera Site|ja|三ツ寺Ⅰ遺跡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mitsudera Site|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} further highlights connections between these clans and the Yamato court.
The Kuramochi clan’s {{ill|Kabane|ja|姓|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kabane|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} was originally "Kimi," later elevated to "Ason" under the {{ill|Eight Prince Ranks|ja|八色の姓|1=Eight Kabane system|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eight Prince Ranks|13=User:Immanuelle/Eight Kabane system|qq=}} reform initiated by {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu|2=ja|3=天武天皇|ja|Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu|2=ja|3=天武天皇|1=Emperor Tenmu|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Tenmu|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Tenmu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q335181}}.<ref>''Nihon Shoki,'' Emperor Tenmu, 13th year, 11th month, 1st day entry.</ref>
== Kuramochi Clan in Wooden Tablets and Registers == * Kuramochi Ason (Name unknown), Kuramochi Ason Michimichi *: Names appear on wooden tablets excavated from the Dazaifu government complex. * Kuramochi no Kimi Nemaro, Kuramochi no Kimi Ikusa, Kuramochi no Kimi Hitsuji *: Appears in the fragmentary register "Bungo Province Nakatsu District Household Registry of 702." * Kuramochi no Sazumaro, Kuramochi no Takan *: Names appear on wooden tablets excavated from the Heijō Palace site. * Kuramochi no Wakamaru *: Appears on labels excavated from the Heijō Palace site.
== References == {{reflist}}
== Sources == * ''Nihon Shoki,'' Volumes 3 and 5, {{ill|Iwanami Bunko|ja|岩波文庫|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwanami Bunko|1=Iwanami Bunko|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11475322}}, 1994–1995. * {{ill|Ujitani Mitsuru|ja|宇治谷孟|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ujitani Mitsuru|qq=}}, ''Nihon Shoki: Complete Modern Translation,'' Vols. 1–2, {{ill|Kōdansha Gakujutsu Bunko|ja|講談社学術文庫|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōdansha Gakujutsu Bunko|1=Kōdansha gakujutsu bunko|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11632220}}, 1988. * {{ill|Shoku Nihongi|en|Shoku Nihongi|WD=Q611399|lt=Shoku Nihongi|1=Shoku Nihongi}} Volumes 1 and 5, {{ill|Iwanami Shoten|en|Iwanami Shoten|WD=Q1331427|lt=Iwanami Shoten|1=Iwanami Shoten}}, 1989, 1998. * {{ill|Saeki Ariyoshi|ja|佐伯有清|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saeki Ariyoshi|qq=}}, ''Japanese Ancient Clan Encyclopedia,'' 2015. * {{ill|Sakamoto Tarō|ja|坂本太郎|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakamoto Tarō|1=Sakamoto Tarō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11425251}}, ''Japanese Ancient Clan Names Dictionary,'' {{ill|Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|ja|吉川弘文館|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|1=Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4346755}}, 1990. * {{ill|Rizō Takeuchi|en|Rizō Takeuchi|WD=Q4262779|lt=Takeuchi Rizo|1=Rizō Takeuchi}}, ''Japanese Ancient Names Dictionary,'' Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1972.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:車持氏]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:車持氏]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:車持氏]]
'''Kuramochi Clan''' (車持氏) is a clan that takes its name, "Kuramochi," from its family lineage. This clan oversaw the occupational group ({{ill|Shinabe clans|en|Shinabe clans|qid=Q11418456|lt=Shinabe|1=Shinabe clans}}) known as the {{ill|Kuramochibe|ja|車持部|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuramochibe|qq=}}, which managed specific duties.
== Overview ==
According to the {{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|en|Shinsen Shōjiroku|qid=Q1045362|lt=Shinsen Shōjiroku|1=Shinsen Shōjiroku}}, under the Left Capital section of imperial lineage, the Kuramochi clan shared the same ancestry as the {{ill|Kamitsukeno clan|ja|上毛野氏|1=Kamitsukeno clan|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamitsukeno clan|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamitsukeno clan|qq=}}. They were descendants of {{ill|Toyokiirihiko-no-mikoto|ja|豊城入彦命|1=Prince Toyokiirihiko|2=ja|3=豊城入彦命|qid=Q11633375|1=Prince Toyokiirihiko|1=Prince Toyokiirihiko|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyokiirihiko-no-mikoto|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Toyokiirihiko|qq=}}, specifically from his eighth-generation descendant Isasa no Kimi. During the reign of {{ill|Emperor Yūryaku|en|Emperor Yūryaku|qid=Q357235|lt=Emperor Yūryaku|1=Emperor Yūryaku}}, the clan earned the surname "Kuramochi no Kimi" after offering vehicles to the court.
The {{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|qid=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki|1=Nihon Shoki}} mentions an event during the 5th year of {{ill|Emperor Richū|en|Emperor Richū|qid=Q329704|lt=Emperor Richū|1=Emperor Richū}} (404 CE, estimated). When Emperor Richū mourned the death of his consort, {{ill|Kurohime|ja|葛城黒媛|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kurohime|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, divine punishment was blamed on Kuramochi no Kimi. He had misused resources assigned to {{ill|Kuramochibe|ja|車持部|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuramochibe|qq=}} for investigations in {{ill|Tsukushi Province|en|Tsukushi Province|qid=Q1206461|lt=Tsukushi Province|1=Tsukushi Province}} and confiscated goods dedicated to {{ill|Munakata Shrine|ja|宗像神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Munakata Shrine|1=Munakata Shrines|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11451876}}. As a result, the emperor condemned Kuramochi no Kimi, ordering purification rites at Nagasunozaki (now Nagasu, {{ill|Amagasaki|en|Amagasaki|qid=Q213318|lt=Amagasaki|1=Amagasaki}}), and banned him from managing Kuramochibe in Tsukushi.<ref>''Nihon Shoki,'' Emperor Richū, 5th year, 10th month, 11th day entry.</ref>
The ''Nihon Shoki,'' citing the "Baekje Annals," records that in the 3rd year of {{ill|Emperor Keitai|en|Emperor Keitai|qid=Q349277|lt=Emperor Keitai|1=Emperor Keitai}} (509), Kuramochi no Kimi (久羅麻致支弥, Kuramachikimi) was sent to [[:en:Baekje|Baekje]] to return refugees who had fled to {{ill|Mimana|en|Mimana|qid=Q1790223|lt=Mimana|1=Mimana}} back to their homeland and re-register them.<ref>{{Cite web|title=久羅麻致支弥|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/久羅麻致支弥-1072490|publisher=Kotobank}}</ref>
During the {{ill|Asuka period|en|Asuka period|qid=Q210688|lt=Asuka period|1=Asuka period}}, Kuramochi Kunikko’s daughter, {{ill|Kuramochi Yoshikome|ja|車持与志古娘|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuramochi Yoshikome|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, married [[:en:Nakatomi no Kamatari|Nakatomi no Kamatari]], giving birth to {{ill|Fujiwara no Fuhito|en|Fujiwara no Fuhito|qid=Q177517|lt=Fujiwara no Fuhito|1=Fujiwara no Fuhito}}.
In the {{ill|Nara period|en|Nara period|qid=Q189178|lt=Nara period|1=Nara period}}, {{ill|Kuramochi Chitose|ja|車持千年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuramochi Chitose|qq=}} served as a court poet for {{ill|Empress Genmei|en|Empress Genmei|qid=Q233224|lt=Empress Genmei|1=Empress Genmei}} and {{ill|Emperor Shōmu|en|Emperor Shōmu|qid=Q349263|lt=Emperor Shōmu|1=Emperor Shōmu}}.
According to the {{ill|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|en|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|qid=Q1052981|lt=Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|1=Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku}}, compiled in 882, the Kuramochi clan served under the {{ill|Traditional Chinese law|en|Traditional Chinese law|qid=Q4380197|lt=Ritsuryō|1=Traditional Chinese law}} system as subordinates in the {{ill|Ministry of the Imperial Household|en|Ministry of the Imperial Household|qid=Q8193428|lt=Ministry of the Imperial Household (Ritsuryō)|1=Ministry of the Imperial Household}}’s {{ill|Shudenryō|ja|主殿寮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shudenryō|qq=}}. Their duties included managing imperial palanquins. The ''Engishiki'' mentions a member of the Kuramochi clan performing ceremonial roles during the {{ill|Daijosai|en|Daijosai|qid=Q11433653|lt=Daijosai|1=Daijosai}}.<ref>''Engishiki,'' vol. 7, ''Shinji,'' "Daijōsai," Article 40.</ref>
The ''Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku'' also states that in 877, the Kuramochi clan and five other clans—Kamitsukeno, Ōno, Ikeda, Sami, and Shimotsukeno—were all descendants of Emperor Sujin. According to the ''Shoku Nihongi,'' a petition submitted by {{ill|Sumiyoshi no Tsunamori|ja|住吉綱主|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sumiyoshi no Tsunamori|qq=}} in 791 noted that these clans were settled in the eastern provinces, taking surnames based on their places of residence. Archaeological evidence from wooden tablets excavated from the Fujiwara Palace indicates that Kamitsukeno’s territory included a district named "Kuramochi District" until the late 7th century.
The {{ill|Hodota Kofun Cluster|en|Hodota Kofun Cluster|qid=Q11385853|lt=Hodota Kofun Group|1=Hodota Kofun Cluster}} in {{ill|Takasaki|en|Takasaki|qid=Q336438|lt=Takasaki|1=Takasaki}}, Gunma Prefecture, includes three keyhole-shaped Kofuns constructed between the late 5th and early 6th centuries. These are believed to be tombs of regional leaders linked to the formation of the Keno Province. The nearby {{ill|Mitsudera Site|ja|三ツ寺Ⅰ遺跡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mitsudera Site|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} further highlights connections between these clans and the Yamato court.
The Kuramochi clan’s {{ill|Kabane|ja|姓|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kabane|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} was originally "Kimi," later elevated to "Ason" under the {{ill|Eight Prince Ranks|ja|八色の姓|1=Eight Kabane system|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eight Prince Ranks|13=User:Immanuelle/Eight Kabane system|qq=}} reform initiated by {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu|2=ja|3=天武天皇|ja|Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu|2=ja|3=天武天皇|1=Emperor Tenmu|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Tenmu|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Tenmu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q335181}}.<ref>''Nihon Shoki,'' Emperor Tenmu, 13th year, 11th month, 1st day entry.</ref>
== Kuramochi Clan in Wooden Tablets and Registers == * Kuramochi Ason (Name unknown), Kuramochi Ason Michimichi *: Names appear on wooden tablets excavated from the Dazaifu government complex. * Kuramochi no Kimi Nemaro, Kuramochi no Kimi Ikusa, Kuramochi no Kimi Hitsuji *: Appears in the fragmentary register "Bungo Province Nakatsu District Household Registry of 702." * Kuramochi no Sazumaro, Kuramochi no Takan *: Names appear on wooden tablets excavated from the Heijō Palace site. * Kuramochi no Wakamaru *: Appears on labels excavated from the Heijō Palace site.
== References == {{reflist}}
== Sources == * ''Nihon Shoki,'' Volumes 3 and 5, {{ill|Iwanami Bunko|ja|岩波文庫|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwanami Bunko|1=Iwanami Bunko|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11475322}}, 1994–1995. * {{ill|Ujitani Mitsuru|ja|宇治谷孟|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ujitani Mitsuru|qq=}}, ''Nihon Shoki: Complete Modern Translation,'' Vols. 1–2, {{ill|Kōdansha Gakujutsu Bunko|ja|講談社学術文庫|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōdansha Gakujutsu Bunko|1=Kōdansha gakujutsu bunko|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11632220}}, 1988. * {{ill|Shoku Nihongi|en|Shoku Nihongi|qid=Q611399|lt=Shoku Nihongi|1=Shoku Nihongi}} Volumes 1 and 5, {{ill|Iwanami Shoten|en|Iwanami Shoten|qid=Q1331427|lt=Iwanami Shoten|1=Iwanami Shoten}}, 1989, 1998. * {{ill|Saeki Ariyoshi|ja|佐伯有清|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saeki Ariyoshi|qq=}}, ''Japanese Ancient Clan Encyclopedia,'' 2015. * {{ill|Sakamoto Tarō|ja|坂本太郎|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakamoto Tarō|1=Sakamoto Tarō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11425251}}, ''Japanese Ancient Clan Names Dictionary,'' {{ill|Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|ja|吉川弘文館|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|1=Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q4346755}}, 1990. * {{ill|Rizō Takeuchi|en|Rizō Takeuchi|qid=Q4262779|lt=Takeuchi Rizo|1=Rizō Takeuchi}}, ''Japanese Ancient Names Dictionary,'' Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1972.

The nearby {{ill|Mitsudera Site|ja|三ツ寺Ⅰ遺跡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mitsudera Site|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} further highlights connections between these clans and the Yamato court.
The Kuramochi clan’s {{ill|Kabane|ja|姓|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kabane|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} was originally "Kimi," later elevated to "Ason" under the {{ill|Eight Prince Ranks|ja|八色の姓|1=Eight Kabane system|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eight Prince Ranks|13=User:Immanuelle/Eight Kabane system|qq=}} reform initiated by {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu|2=ja|3=天武天皇|ja|Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu|2=ja|3=天武天皇|1=Emperor Tenmu|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Tenmu|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Tenmu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q335181}}.<ref>''Nihon Shoki,'' Emperor Tenmu, 13th year, 11th month, 1st day entry.</ref>
== Kuramochi Clan in Wooden Tablets and Registers == * Kuramochi Ason (Name unknown), Kuramochi Ason Michimichi *: Names appear on wooden tablets excavated from the Dazaifu government complex. * Kuramochi no Kimi Nemaro, Kuramochi no Kimi Ikusa, Kuramochi no Kimi Hitsuji *: Appears in the fragmentary register "Bungo Province Nakatsu District Household Registry of 702." * Kuramochi no Sazumaro, Kuramochi no Takan *: Names appear on wooden tablets excavated from the Heijō Palace site. * Kuramochi no Wakamaru *: Appears on labels excavated from the Heijō Palace site.
